653	/**
654	 * True when `view` is NOT the real, top-level note editor but a separate,
655	 * nested CM6 instance Obsidian mounts inside another widget's own DOM — the
656	 * only case found so far: a table cell currently being edited in Live
657	 * Preview renders as its own tiny, independent `EditorView` embedded inside
658	 * `.cm-embed-block.cm-table-widget` (confirmed live by walking the DOM
659	 * ancestry of a stray marker up to the table widget). `registerEditorExtension`
660	 * (main.ts) applies this whole extension to EVERY CM6 instance app-wide,
661	 * this nested one included, and its own "document" is just the cell's raw
662	 * text — a bare line with no special syntax reads as a plain paragraph to
663	 * decorate()/parse(), so without this guard it picks up a marker AND
664	 * depth-based padding/margin exactly like a real top-level paragraph,
665	 * visibly corrupting the cell being edited. A real top-level note's own
666	 * `.cm-editor` is never itself nested inside a `.cm-embed-block` (those are
667	 * its own descendants, not its ancestors), so this only ever fires for a
668	 * genuinely embedded editor — confirmed also via `editorInfoField`, which
669	 * resolves to the SAME outer `MarkdownView` for both, so state alone can't
670	 * tell them apart; only the DOM ancestry can, which is why this check lives
671	 * here (view-level) rather than in the state-only decoration builders.
672	 */
673	function isNestedEditor(view: EditorView): boolean {
674	  return view.dom.closest('.cm-embed-block') !== null;
675	}
676	
677	class DecorationsPlugin implements PluginValue {
678	  decorations: DecorationSet;
679	
680	  constructor(
681	    private readonly view: EditorView,
682	    private readonly modes: DecorationSource,
683	  ) {
684	    this.decorations = this.compute();
685	  }
686	
687	  update(): void {
688	    this.decorations = this.compute();
689	  }
690	
691	  private compute(): DecorationSet {
692	    if (isNestedEditor(this.view)) return Decoration.none;
693	    return computeDecorations(this.view.state, this.modes);
694	  }
695	}
696	
697	class MarkersPlugin implements PluginValue {
698	  decorations: DecorationSet;
699	
700	  constructor(
701	    private readonly view: EditorView,
702	    private readonly modes: DecorationSource,
703	  ) {
704	    this.decorations = this.compute();
705	  }
706	
707	  update(): void {
708	    this.decorations = this.compute();
709	  }
710	
711	  private compute(): DecorationSet {
712	    if (isNestedEditor(this.view)) return Decoration.none;
713	    return computeMarkers(this.view.state, this.modes);
714	  }
715	}
716	
717	class MarginCompensation implements PluginValue {
718	  constructor(
719	    private readonly view: EditorView,
720	    private readonly modes: DecorationSource,
721	  ) {
722	    this.apply();
723	  }
724	
725	  docViewUpdate(): void {
726	    this.apply();
727	  }
728	
729	  destroy(): void {
730	    this.clearAll();
731	  }
732	
733	  /**
734	   * The margin-left every undecorated `.cm-line` gets natively (Obsidian's
735	   * "readable line width" centering, `margin-inline: auto` under a
736	   * `max-width` — see the module doc comment). Read live, not hardcoded or
737	   * replicated via a calc() formula, since its value depends on the
738	   * current theme/viewport/setting and isn't exposed as a plain-length CSS
739	   * custom property we could reference (`--content-margin`'s own
740	   * *specified* value is literally the keyword `auto`, not a length).
741	   * `.to-decor-block` lines never have their own margin-left touched (they
742	   * use padding), so any one of them is an uncontaminated reference; a
743	   * completely undecorated line (blank gap, preamble) works just as well.
744	   * Falls back to 0 only if the current viewport has neither (e.g. a
745	   * document that is 100% margin-decorated content with nothing else
746	   * rendered) — a graceful degradation, not silently wrong in the common
747	   * case.
748	   */
749	  private nativeMarginBasePx(): number {
750	    // `.hr` is excluded too: it carries `.cm-line` but is widget-rendered
751	    // (see WIDGET_ATOM_SELECTOR) and patched by the loop below — if a
752	    // previous `apply()` call already set its margin-left, querying it
753	    // here would read back our OWN prior value, not the native one.
754	    const ref = this.view.contentDOM.querySelector<HTMLElement>(
755	      `.cm-line:not(.to-decor-atom):not(.to-decor-list):not(.hr)`,
756	    );
757	    return ref ? parseFloat(getComputedStyle(ref).marginLeft) || 0 : 0;
758	  }
759	
760	  /**
761	   * Live measurement for the fold-chevron repositioning transform
762	   * (styles.css) — hardening 5.1, replacing two hardcoded measured
763	   * constants with the same read-native-values-live pattern
764	   * `nativeMarginBasePx` establishes: measure one representative element
765	   * per render, apply uniformly via a custom property on the content DOM
766	   * (custom properties inherit, so every chevron's transform picks it up).
767	   *
768	   * What the transform needs (see styles.css's own comment for the full
769	   * spatial story): shift = gutter + half our marker icon's width + a small
770	   * visual gap − the chevron's own right-side DEAD SPACE (the invisible
771	   * hit-area padding between its `.collapse-indicator` box's right edge and
772	   * the painted `<svg>` glyph's right edge — ~6px in the bundled themes,
773	   * but native Obsidian sizing that a theme/Obsidian update can change,
774	   * which is exactly why it must be measured, not hardcoded). Only the dead
775	   * space is a native measurement; the gutter and icon size are our own
776	   * constants, threaded from their single JS source of truth
777	   * (`MARKER_GUTTER_CSS`/`MARKER_ICON_CSS`) per the shared-value lesson.
778	   *
779	   * Measures `.collapse-indicator` (the element that actually carries the
780	   * box width) against its own painted `<svg>` — NOT the `.cm-fold-indicator`
781	   * wrapper, which is a zero-width anchor whose rect is technically true but
782	   * practically useless (the measure-the-glyph-not-the-wrapper lesson,
783	   * 11-decoration-lessons.md). A width DIFFERENCE is translation-invariant,
784	   * so measuring an already-transformed chevron still yields the correct
785	   * dead space — no untransformed-position bookkeeping needed. When no
786	   * chevron is currently rendered (nothing foldable in the viewport), the
787	   * last measurement — or, before any, the CSS fallback matching the
788	   * previously-validated bundled-theme values — stays in effect.
789	   *
790	   * The property is written to `view.dom` (the outer `.cm-editor`), NOT
791	   * `view.contentDOM`, and only when the (rounded) value actually changed —
792	   * both preventive, chosen deliberately rather than in response to an
793	   * observed failure. CM6's DOMObserver observes `contentDOM` itself with
794	   * `attributes: true` (confirmed in @codemirror/view's source), so a
795	   * style-attribute write there lands inside the observed set; and this
796	   * measurement is a rect difference on a transformed element, which could
797	   * in principle jitter subpixel between renders. A value that keeps
798	   * changing, written to an observed attribute, from a hook the resulting
799	   * mutation might re-trigger, is the same mutation-observer feedback-loop
800	   * family as the module doc comment's "never append a child into a plain
801	   * `.cm-line`" invariant — staying outside the observed subtree removes
802	   * the question entirely, at zero cost (custom properties inherit, so
803	   * `view.dom` serves the descendant chevron rule just as well).
804	   */
805	  private lastDeadRight = '';
806	
807	  private measureChevron(): void {
808	    const wrapper = this.view.contentDOM.querySelector<HTMLElement>(
809	      '.cm-fold-indicator .collapse-indicator',
810	    );
811	    const glyph = wrapper?.querySelector('svg');
812	    if (!wrapper || !glyph) return;
813	    const wrapperRect = wrapper.getBoundingClientRect();
814	    const glyphRect = glyph.getBoundingClientRect();
815	    if (wrapperRect.width === 0 || glyphRect.width === 0) return;
816	    const deadRight = `${(wrapperRect.right - glyphRect.right).toFixed(1)}px`;
817	    if (deadRight === this.lastDeadRight) return;
818	    this.lastDeadRight = deadRight;
819	    this.view.dom.setCssProps({ '--to-chevron-dead-right': deadRight });
820	  }
821	
822	  private apply(): void {
823	    const path = this.view.state.field(editorInfoField, false)?.file?.path;
824	    // See isNestedEditor's own doc comment — a nested per-cell editor
825	    // shares this.modes.isOutline's own path with the real top-level note,
826	    // so that check alone can't exclude it; only the DOM-level one can.
827	    if (!path || !this.modes.isOutline(path) || isNestedEditor(this.view)) {
828	      this.clearAll();
829	      return;
830	    }
831	
832	    // The chevron transform's inputs: our own icon size, threaded from its
833	    // JS source of truth (a constant, so writing it repeatedly is safe),
834	    // plus the live-measured native dead space (see measureChevron — set on
835	    // `view.dom`, not `contentDOM`, for the observer-loop reason documented
836	    // there; same target here for consistency). Re-measured every render,
837	    // so a theme switch mid-session corrects on the next update.
838	    this.view.dom.setCssProps({ '--to-marker-icon-size': MARKER_ICON_CSS });
839	    this.measureChevron();
840	
841	    const { factsByLine, guidesByLine } = docFacts(this.view.state);
842	    const nativeBasePx = this.nativeMarginBasePx();
843	
844	    const widgets = Array.from(
845	      this.view.contentDOM.querySelectorAll<HTMLElement>(WIDGET_ATOM_SELECTOR),
846	    );
847	    for (const el of widgets) {
848	      const lineNumber = this.view.state.doc.lineAt(this.view.posAtDOM(el)).number - 1;
849	      const fact = factsByLine.get(lineNumber);
850	      if (fact?.isAtom) {
851	        // Some widgets (tables, for their row/column drag-handles) carry
852	        // their own native left padding that our margin doesn't know
853	        // about — padding never moves a box's own edge, so it just pushes
854	        // the widget's *visible content* (e.g. the <table> grid) further
855	        // right than a same-depth code block or callout, whose background
856	        // fills their own padding invisibly. Reading it live (not a
857	        // hardcoded constant) keeps this correct across themes; clamped at
858	        // 0 so a depth-0 atom never goes negative.
859	        const nativePaddingLeft = parseFloat(getComputedStyle(el).paddingLeft) || 0;
860	        // The widget's own box's rightward shift *from our own
861	        // contribution alone* — this (NOT including nativeBasePx) is also
862	        // the exact compensation a guide's pseudo-element needs to widen
863	        // itself by, leftward, to reach a shallower ancestor's column (see
864	        // the "Guide lines" doc comment above): nativeBasePx applies
865	        // uniformly to every line regardless of depth, so it cancels out
866	        // of the *difference* between any two lines' columns and must be
867	        // added to margin-left but never to `--to-own-shift`. Includes the
868	        // marker gutter (Experiment 5a) — every widget atom always gets a
869	        // marker, so its own box is always shifted by at least the gutter,
870	        // even at depth 0. `applyWidgetMarker` below derives the marker's
871	        // OWN target column from this exact same expression, so the two can
872	        // never silently diverge again.
873	        const ownShiftExpr = `max(0px, calc(${fact.depth} * ${UNIT} - ${nativePaddingLeft}px)) + ${MARKER_GUTTER_CSS}`;
874	        el.style.setProperty('margin-left', `calc(${nativeBasePx}px + ${ownShiftExpr})`, 'important');
875	
876	        // The gutter reservation above stays unconditional regardless of
877	        // markerVisibility — hiding some markers should never reflow text
878	        // or shift indentation, only whether the icon itself is drawn in
879	        // that already-reserved space (see shouldShowMarker's own doc
880	        // comment).
881	        if (shouldShowMarker(fact, this.modes.markerVisibility)) {
882	          el.classList.add('to-decor-marker');
883	          el.dataset.markerDepth = String(fact.depth);
884	          applyWidgetMarker(el, fact.kind, ownShiftExpr);
885	        } else {
886	          clearWidgetMarker(el);
887	        }
888	
889	        const guide = guidesByLine.get(lineNumber);
890	        if (guide && guide.guideDepths.length > 0) {
891	          el.classList.add('to-decor-guides');
892	          el.style.setProperty('--to-guides', guideBackground(guide.guideDepths));
893	          el.style.setProperty('--to-own-shift', `calc(${ownShiftExpr})`);
894	        } else {
895	          el.classList.remove('to-decor-guides');
896	          el.style.removeProperty('--to-guides');
897	          el.style.removeProperty('--to-own-shift');
898	        }
899	      } else {
900	        el.style.removeProperty('margin-left');
901	        el.classList.remove('to-decor-guides');
902	        el.style.removeProperty('--to-guides');
903	        el.style.removeProperty('--to-own-shift');
904	        clearWidgetMarker(el);
905	      }
906	    }
907	
908	    // Plain lines (atoms/list items rendered as genuine `.cm-line`s, not
909	    // widgets): styles.css's static `calc(depth * unit) !important` rule
910	    // already sets the class-driven part correctly, but has no way to
911	    // read/add nativeBasePx (a StateField has no DOM to measure — only a
912	    // ViewPlugin, running after render, can). This overrides it inline
913	    // (inline `!important` beats any stylesheet `!important`, regardless
914	    // of specificity) with the same value PLUS the live-read native base.
915	    const plainLines = Array.from(
916	      this.view.contentDOM.querySelectorAll<HTMLElement>(PLAIN_MARGIN_SELECTOR),
917	    );
918	    for (const el of plainLines) {
919	      const isListItem = el.classList.contains('to-decor-list');
920	      const depthVar = isListItem ? '--to-supp-depth' : '--to-depth';
921	      // List items get no marker gutter (native bullet/number only, no
922	      // icon); atom lines (code/quote) always reserve one, even at depth 0.
923	      const gutter = isListItem ? '0px' : MARKER_GUTTER_CSS;
924	      el.style.setProperty(
925	        'margin-left',
926	        `calc(${nativeBasePx}px + var(${depthVar}, 0) * ${UNIT} + ${gutter})`,
927	        'important',
928	      );
929	
930	      // Marker horizontal compensation: `computeMarkers`'s own `left` calc
931	      // (via MARKER_LEFT_SHIFT_EXPR) assumes the widget's insertion point has
932	      // zero native rightward shift on this line — true for a plain
933	      // paragraph, but not for two atom-plain cases, both live-verified:
934	      //
935	      // - A code fence's own opener line (`.HyperMD-codeblock-begin`)
936	      //   carries plain native `padding-left` (confirmed live: 16px in
937	      //   bundled themes, presumably reserved for the language-label
938	      //   pill) with no offsetting `text-indent` — shifts the insertion
939	      //   point right by the full padding amount.
940	      // - A blockquote's own line carries the SAME hanging-indent PAIR
941	      //   the original postmortem already flagged for list-item bullets
942	      //   (`text-indent: -13px` matched with `padding-left: 13px`,
943	      //   confirmed live) — the negative text-indent cancels the padding
944	      //   for the line's own FIRST inline position, so naively
945	      //   subtracting `padding-left` alone overcorrects by the full
946	      //   padding amount (confirmed live: produced a new, equal-and-
947	      //   opposite misalignment). Summing `padding-left + text-indent`
948	      //   (text-indent already negative when present) gives the correct
949	      //   net shift in both cases: 16px + 0 for code, 13px + -13px = 0
950	      //   for quote.
951	      //
952	      // Same class of bug the widget-atom fix above already handles for
953	      // tables (a native offset our own formula doesn't know about) — same
954	      // fix here: read it live, never hardcode it, since it depends on the
955	      // active theme. A `querySelector` (not a `decorate()` fact lookup)
956	      // gates this: only a line `computeMarkers` actually placed an icon
957	      // on has one to correct.
958	      const icon = el.querySelector<HTMLElement>(':scope > .to-decor-marker-icon');
959	      if (icon) {
960	        const iconLineStyle = getComputedStyle(el);
961	        const nativeShift =
962	          (parseFloat(iconLineStyle.paddingLeft) || 0) + (parseFloat(iconLineStyle.textIndent) || 0);
963	        // Always SET (never remove) `left` here: this element is the SAME
964	        // node `MarkerWidget.toDOM()` already applied its own base
965	        // `left` to (in the same inline-style object) — `removeProperty`
966	        // would delete that value entirely (falling back to `auto`, i.e.
967	        // no shift at all) instead of restoring it, a real bug caught live
968	        // when a blockquote's own padding/text-indent pair summed to
969	        // exactly 0 and the marker landed with no shift whatsoever.
970	        icon.style.setProperty(
971	          'left',
972	          nativeShift !== 0
973	            ? `calc(${MARKER_LEFT_SHIFT_EXPR} - ${nativeShift}px)`
974	            : MARKER_LEFT_SHIFT_EXPR,
975	          'important',
976	        );
977	      }
978	    }
979	  }
980	
981	  private clearAll(): void {
982	    this.view.dom.style.removeProperty('--to-marker-icon-size');
983	    this.view.dom.style.removeProperty('--to-chevron-dead-right');
984	    this.lastDeadRight = '';
985	    const widgets = Array.from(
986	      this.view.contentDOM.querySelectorAll<HTMLElement>(WIDGET_ATOM_SELECTOR),
987	    );
988	    for (const el of widgets) {
989	      el.style.removeProperty('margin-left');
990	      el.classList.remove('to-decor-guides');
991	      el.style.removeProperty('--to-guides');
992	      el.style.removeProperty('--to-own-shift');
993	      clearWidgetMarker(el);
994	    }
995	    const plainLines = Array.from(
996	      this.view.contentDOM.querySelectorAll<HTMLElement>(PLAIN_MARGIN_SELECTOR),
997	    );
998	    for (const el of plainLines) el.style.removeProperty('margin-left');
999	  }
1000	}
1001	
1002	export function decorationsExtension(modes: DecorationSource): Extension {
1003	  return [
1004	    // ViewPlugins (not plain StateFields) specifically so each has `view`
1005	    // access to run isNestedEditor's DOM-ancestry check — state alone can't
1006	    // tell a nested per-cell editor apart from the real top-level note (see
1007	    // isNestedEditor's own doc comment). Recomputes on every update, not
1008	    // just docChanged ones: toggling outline mode has no doc change of its
1009	    // own, only a nudged selection transaction (see main.ts) to make these
1010	    // re-run.
1011	    ViewPlugin.define((view) => new DecorationsPlugin(view, modes), {
1012	      decorations: (v) => v.decorations,
1013	    }),
1014	    // A SEPARATE plugin for block-marker widgets (Experiment 5a), not
1015	    // merged into the same RangeSetBuilder as the line decorations above —
1016	    // CM6 merges decorations from multiple sources correctly on its own,
1017	    // sidestepping any need to reason about Decoration.line/Decoration.
1018	    // widget ordering at the same document position.
1019	    ViewPlugin.define((view) => new MarkersPlugin(view, modes), {
1020	      decorations: (v) => v.decorations,
1021	    }),
1022	    ViewPlugin.define<MarginCompensation>((view) => new MarginCompensation(view, modes)),
1023	  ];
1024	}
1025

content.thinking:
I've got enough context now to move forward. I'll set up my internal task tracking aligned with the groups in tasks.md, then start implementing section 1 with the pure classifier core.

I'm defining the TransactionFacts interface to capture the key data needed for classification: the userEvent annotation, composition state, change ranges over the document, and selection information. This will feed into the six-class taxonomy that determines whether an edit is programmatic, in composition, plugin-owned, selection-only, within a single node, or crossing node boundaries.

Defining the TransactionFacts interface... Checking the actual code paths for the 'continue' operation to see which userEvent values are being used across different node types, and noticing that both list-item continuation and heading splits are using 'input.structure.split' rather than the generic 'input' I initially thought. Writing handler logic...

I'm noticing that the 'continue' operation uses a generic 'input' event marker instead of a distinguishing one, which creates a problem for identifying plugin-specific actions. Structural commands created through `editor.transaction()` don't explicitly set a userEvent, so they'd be classified as programmatic transactions under the D2 ordering. This actually works out — since programmatic transactions pass through untouched just like plugin-own ones do, the behavior is identical even though the taxonomy treats them differently.

The grammar keymap actions (Tab, Shift-Tab, Alt-Up/Down, Enter, Shift-Enter) that use userEvent annotations like 'input.structure.indent' are properly recognized as plugin-own. But the 'continue' action triggered by Shift-Enter uses a plain 'input' userEvent instead of a structure-specific one, which is the outlier in how it's annotated compared to the other structural commands. I should note this for the Phase A findings about the userEvent values I'm observing. Now I'm checking how Obsidian's editor.transaction method works — specifically whether it passes a userEvent field when dispatching to CodeMirror, and I need to look at the type definitions to understand if structural commands get annotated differently than user input.

When main.ts calls `editor.transaction({ changes })` without specifying an origin, no userEvent gets set, so structural commands fall into the 'programmatic' classification by the classifier. This is still safe since 'programmatic' passes through the same way as 'plugin-own', though the spec has specific requirements around this.

The grammar-dispatched Tab indent scenario explicitly requires 'plugin-own' classification because it uses CM6's view.dispatch with a userEvent set, but the command palette's structural commands take a different path through main.ts's high-level API. This way, even if we add sub-namespaced events later like `input.structure.indent.something`, they'll still be recognized as plugin-owned.

For the "selection-only" case, I'm checking whether there are no document changes by looking at `!facts.hasChanges`. For class 4 (selection-only), I just need to check that there are no document changes — anything that doesn't match the earlier classes falls here. Then for classes 5 and 6, which only apply when there are actual changes, I'm comparing the changed line ranges against the tree node spans to distinguish between edits contained within a single node versus those crossing node boundaries.

I need to implement node-span resolution to map line ranges to their containing nodes. I'm thinking about whether to extend the existing locate.ts utilities or build this directly into the classify logic, and I need to clarify how to handle gap lines when determining which node a line belongs to.

For change ranges with fromLine and toLine, I need to resolve both endpoints to their respective nodes—treating the case where fromLine equals toLine as a zero-width insertion at that line boundary rather than a replacement. Since the classify module receives CM6 changes translated into line-range facts by the adapter, I'm working out what the shape of these line spans should be: likely a simple `{fromLine, toLine}` structure derived from the character offsets.

The key question is which tree to use for classification—the startState's tree (before the transaction) makes sense since the change ranges describe pre-change document line spans, keeping everything consistent with how node-span resolution works. For selection-only transactions where the document is unchanged, the startState's tree is the same as the current state's tree anyway. the old document's parse tree, since we're classifying what the change touched rather than its result. I'll iterate through the changed ranges using the old positions (fromA/toA) and convert them to line numbers in the pre-edit document.

For the line range calculation, I need to be careful about the semantics — fromLine should be 0-based, and toLine should follow the exclusive convention matching the Edit type. When toA equals fromA (a pure insertion with no deletion), I need to figure out which line the inserted content belongs to for span-checking purposes. When a pure insertion happens at a node boundary, it still only touches the single line where the insertion occurs, so the classification only needs to check nodes within that line's range — the ambiguity of which side of the boundary the insertion belongs to doesn't matter since it affects just one line anyway.

For actual deletions or replacements spanning multiple lines, I need to compute the starting line number and then determine the ending line based on the range.  The key insight is that since `toA` is exclusive, I should look at `toA - 1` to find the last character actually affected. Using `Math.max(fromA, toA - 1)` handles both insertions (where `toA === fromA`) and deletions uniformly — insertions naturally resolve to the same line, while deletions correctly identify the final affected line without needing special cases.

Now I'm defining a `LineSpan` type with `fromLine` and `toLine` as inclusive bounds on both ends, which is cleaner than the exclusive convention used elsewhere and avoids off-by-one errors when classifying changes. A change crosses a boundary when the starting and ending lines belong to different nodes, or when one of them falls in the preamble. So the definition becomes: a within-node-edit means every change range stays within a single node or all within preamble (same identity), while a boundary-crossing-edit has at least one range that spans different identities. I need to apply this consistently across all change ranges in a transaction—if even one range crosses a boundary, the whole transaction is boundary-crossing.

For multi-node ranges themselves, comparing nodeAtLine for both endpoints handles it correctly: if they resolve to the same node, it's within; if different nodes, it's crossing, regardless of any gap lines in between. This catches cases where a deletion spans from mid-node A through mid-node B as a boundary-crossing, which is the right behavior.

Now I need to think about the "node span resolution" API. I'm considering adding a small utility to locate.ts or classify.ts that can resolve either a node ID, the 'preamble' sentinel, or undefined. Since classify.ts is meant to stay free of Obsidian and CM6 dependencies, and locate.ts already lives in src/plugin/ without CM6 imports, I should check whether the resolver belongs in the pure classifier module (mirroring the ops.ts/dispatch.ts pattern in src/) or if locate.ts can handle it despite being in src/plugin/.

content.thinking:
Now I'm writing the test suite for the classification logic, covering all reachable classes, verifying that each input maps to exactly one class, testing the precedence rules where composition takes priority over plugin-own events and programmatic wins when there's no userEvent, and ensuring unknown factories default to permit.

I'm also testing edge cases with unrecognized userEvents—those without changes should classify as selection-only, while those with changes should be classified based on the tree structure as within or boundary—plus handling gap lines and preamble boundaries.

I'll construct a test document with headings, paragraphs, and list items, then parse it and verify that nodeAtLine correctly resolves each line to its owning node by checking the span calculations. So the list item attaches to the preceding paragraph as a child regardless of the blank line separating them—the parser checks the immediate sibling in the container's children array and always attaches to a paragraph if that's what precedes it. The 'Para two.' node carries its own trailingGap value. Blank-line ownership and child relationships are independent concepts, which aligns with the design principle that blank lines belong to the preceding node. When I trace through nodeAtLine, Para two ends at line 6, so checking line 5 correctly finds it. Then for line 6 where the list item sits, I need to descend into Para two's children since line 6 isn't less than the end boundary, and that walk finds the list item node properly. This confirms the layout structure I had in mind.

Now I'm setting up test cases with this line-to-node mapping, including a preamble example with frontmatter and blank lines, plus a boundary-crossing scenario where a span crosses from one node to another to verify they're treated as different nodes.

Writing test cases... Since this is Phase A where document rewrites never happen regardless of classification, the preamble-crossing choice doesn't affect behavior—it's just a categorization decision. Spans entirely within the preamble should be classified as within-node-edits since both endpoints resolve to the same identity. I should verify this with a property test across arbitrary documents and transaction combinations to ensure the classifier always returns exactly one class.

Now I need to establish the classification invariants: programmatic edits when userEvent is undefined, selection-only edits when there are no changed lines and userEvent is a custom string outside the standard events, and composition taking precedence over plugin-own events when isComposition is true. Let me write the test suite now.

content.thinking:
I'm setting up a test file for the escalate function with unit and property-based tests covering idempotence, orientation preservation, and ensuring that content within nodes, cursors, and preambles stays unchanged along with subtree boundaries. I'm starting with a test document that has a heading, paragraphs, and nested list items to work through the parsing logic manually.

Now I'm tracing through how the parser attaches these elements: the list item at indent 0 gets attached as a child of the preceding paragraph (Para two) based on the attachment rule in parse.ts, which checks if the previous sibling is a paragraph regardless of indentation. The nested child list item then becomes a child of the parent list item, creating a tree structure where Para two contains the list item, which in turn contains its own child.

With the paths established, I'm working through test scenarios: selections entirely within one paragraph remain unchanged, cursor positions (where anchor equals head) are unchanged, and when dragging across paragraphs I need to find the common ancestor by comparing path indices until they diverge. For a selection from mid Para-one to mid Para-two, the divergence happens at index 1, making the heading the scope node with both paragraphs as its children, then I calculate the range of affected children using the anchor and head indices.

Now I'm computing the actual line and character positions for the selection boundaries: the start position comes from the first subtree (Para one), and the end position requires recursively traversing through Para two's nested structure—accounting for its own lines, trailing gaps, and children—until reaching the deepest child node to determine the final line number.

Since the range isn't backward (the head at line 4 is after the anchor at line 2), the result correctly places the anchor at the start and head at the end. Now checking what happens when the drag direction reverses.

For a backward drag where the head is at line 2 and anchor at line 4, the escalation logic applies symmetrically—the same subtrees get selected but the positions swap, so the anchor ends up at the higher position (line 7) and the head at the lower position (line 2), which matches the spec's requirement that backward selections keep the head at the start side.

Moving on to test a selection that starts inside heading text and ends inside a paragraph. Both indices land on the same element, so the selection escalates to the full subtree of that heading, spanning from its start all the way through its deepest descendant—which matches the expected behavior for a selection that leaves a parent and covers its entire subtree. Now I should verify this holds up under property-based testing with generated trees, and check that the algorithm works correctly regardless of selection orientation, handles preamble content, and scales across multiple siblings. I need to set up a property test that picks two random resolvable lines from the generated tree and constructs a range with them, then verifies that escalation preserves the backward/forward orientation when the range actually changes, or trivially preserves it when unchanged. I'll enumerate all valid node-owned lines by checking which line indices resolve to actual nodes, then randomly select two of them to form the anchor and head positions.

For the escalation verification, I also need to confirm that when a range does escalate, both boundaries snap to subtree edges: the anchor position's column must be 0 and fall on a node's start line, while the head position's column must equal the line length at that position, marking the end of some node's content.

Writing comprehensive tests...

content.thinking:
I'm recalculating the line numbers for the multi-section test case—my expected count was off by one, so I need to recount the actual lines in the markdown string to verify the correct output. Now I'm tracing through the nodeAtLine walk logic: when checking line 0 against the One node, its end boundary is calculated as the heading's own line count plus its trailing gap, which gives us line 0 and 1 together. The walk finds the line within that range but doesn't descend into children—it just returns the heading node itself, since the gap calculation bundles the heading and its following blank line as a single unit. not < end(2)? NO (2<2 is false), so current updates to 2 and we descend into One's children to search for line 2 within Body-one's span. When we find it there (end=4, so 2<4 is true), found gets set to Body-one and we return. The key insight is that once found is truthy, the outer forEach loop stops calling walk on the remaining siblings like Two and Three, so we correctly resolve line 2 to the Body-one node rather than continuing the search. One's frame doesn't propagate back up through the forEach loop—each call to nodeAtLine starts fresh with current reset to 0, so when we check line 4 (Two's line), we're walking the tree again from the root, and the current variable gets updated independently within that new traversal context. The closure correctly shares the `current` variable across the entire recursive traversal, incrementing it monotonically as we walk through the document in pre-order, which accumulates the total lines consumed by all preceding subtrees. This matches the tested behavior already used throughout the codebase, so I can trust it fully. Now let me recompute the full document for the multi-sibling test case using the actual line numbers. I see the issue now—my test was checking the wrong line number. The actual result correctly identifies line 6, character 9 as the end position for the 'Body two.' paragraph, since that's where the content ends on its own line without any child nodes attached.

Now I'm hitting a different test failure with the property that checks whether escalated ranges always start at character 0 and end at a line's full length. The counterexample involves a complex nested structure with headings containing paragraphs with deeply nested list items, and the escalation isn't producing the expected boundary behavior.

The core question is whether this is a genuine bug or if my assumption about range endpoints is wrong in some edge case. The `subtreeContentEnd` function should compute the end position based on the deepest last descendant's own last line length, which should be correct by construction — but there might be a mismatch between how the test reconstructs the lines array versus how the internal computation actually works.

I should write a minimal reproduction script using the counterexample tree to debug this directly rather than reasoning through it abstractly. — so when both positions land on the same line with different character offsets, my lo/hi selection based purely on line comparison treats them as equal, causing the ternary to arbitrarily pick one as the lower bound. This means if anchor has ch=0 and head has ch=length, the algorithm correctly identifies anchor as the start position even though they're technically on the same line.

The real issue is in my test's lo/hi picker — it's only comparing `.line` values without falling back to `.ch` when lines match, which doesn't align with how escalateRange actually determines position ordering. I need to fix the test to use full position-based comparison (line then ch) to properly verify the counterexample. When the anchor node is an ancestor of the head node, both subtrees map to the same index, so the range could still be single-line if that ancestor node itself spans only one line. So if both firstSubtree and lastSubtree are single-line leaf nodes in a genuine sibling range, that's a different scenario to check—whether start.line could equal end.line in that case.

Actually, I think I need to just write a test to see what's actually happening with a concrete counterexample rather than keep reasoning through the possibilities.

content.thinking:
I'm working through the tree structure logic—node id5 has two children (id6 and id8), and I'm calculating where the subtree content ends by accumulating line counts and recursing through the children to find the final boundary.

The issue is becoming clear: when I sum up the preceding siblings' line spans to compute the start line for the last child, I'm only counting each sibling's own lines and trailing gap, not accounting for their descendants. Node id6 has a nested child (id7), so its actual total span is larger than just its own lines plus gap. I need to calculate the full subtree size for each preceding sibling, not just treat them as leaves.

I'm defining a helper function that recursively counts a node's total line span by summing its own lines and gap, then adding up the subtree counts of all its children. Then in the loop where I compute the start line for the last child, I'll use this subtree count instead of just the sibling's direct lines and gap.

Writing the corrected function...

Now I'm manually verifying the logic with node5: its children are nodes 6 and 8, so I walk through node6's subtree (which includes node7 as a child), accumulating 2 lines total, then pass that sum as the offset when recursing into node8. The final result correctly points to line 9, column 5, matching the expected position.

Let me also check `startLineOf` — that function does a full document walk threading the line counter through a closure, which is the same pattern used in `nodeAtLine` and already proven correct, so it doesn't have this bug. The issue was specific to `subtreeContentEnd`'s sibling loop, which wasn't accounting for descendants nested within earlier siblings.

content.thinking:
I'm setting up the transaction-filter adapter with outline mode gating through the editorInfoField, then extracting key facts like the user event type, composition state, and changed line spans from the transaction changes, before accessing the parsed document.

For selection-only classifications, I need to iterate through the transaction's new selection (the selection this transaction is about to apply) and escalate each range by converting between CM6 offsets and LinePos coordinates. lection to downstream handlers. Since `tr.newSelection` always reflects the resulting selection state (either explicitly set or unchanged from the prior state), using it uniformly is safe and idempotent — even if a selection-only transaction doesn't intend a new selection, feeding an already-consistent selection through is a no-op.

Now I'm building the escalated EditorSelection by mapping each range through the escalation logic, then reconstructing it with the main index preserved. I'll need to import EditorSelection and SelectionRange from @codemirror/state. I'll only wrap the result in a transaction object if the ranges actually changed positions; otherwise return the transaction as-is to avoid unnecessary overhead.

Finally, I'm measuring the execution time and recording it to stats, with debug logging gated behind a flag.

For converting between CodeMirror's character offsets and line/column positions, I'm using the document's lineAt method to get line metadata, then calculating the column offset from the position. Going the other direction, I'll look up the line and add the column offset to its starting position.

To extract which lines changed, I'm iterating through the ChangeSet's changed ranges against the before-state document to identify the affected line spans.

Now I need to handle EditorSelection ranges, which have anchor and head offsets plus an empty flag, and design how to handle multiple selection ranges when escalating per range.

I'll organize the helper functions for fact extraction and escalation application as separate utilities within the transaction filter, keeping the logic clear. Since escalation only applies to selection-only transactions per the spec, other transaction classes leave their selections unchanged. The performance budget covers classification cost including tree access, so I need to be mindful of that constraint when implementing the escalation logic.

For timing, I'll measure the entire filter callback as one unit—fact extraction, parsing, classification, and escalation if applicable—and record it under the appropriate class bucket. This gives the most accurate picture of per-transaction cost and aligns with how task 3.6 will measure performance during typing and selection interactions.

Now I need to set up the extension itself using `EditorState.transactionFilter.of()` and import the necessary types from `@codemirror/state`.

Before doing any work, I'll gate on outline mode first—if it's off, the extension exits immediately with no stats recorded or side effects, keeping performance indistinguishable from stock Obsidian per the spec.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Tasks: outline-selection-enforcement

## 1. Pure classifier core (Phase A foundation)

- [ ] 1.1 Define the transaction-facts data shape and the six-class taxonomy in a new
      pure module `src/classify.ts` (no CM6/Obsidian imports): `classify(facts, doc)`
      evaluating classes in the D2 order, default-permit
- [ ] 1.2 Implement node-span resolution for change ranges and selection ends over
      `OutlineDoc` (reusing/extending `src/plugin/locate.ts` line→node resolution;
      decide whether `nodeAtLine` moves into core or classify takes a resolver)
- [ ] 1.3 Unit + property tests for the classifier: every class reachable, exactly one
      class per input, order precedence, default-permit on unknown facts, gap-line and
      preamble edge cases

## 2. Funnel adapter and instrumentation (Phase A wiring)

- [ ] 2.1 Add the `parsedDoc(doc)` WeakMap parse cache module with timing capture
- [ ] 2.2 Implement the `transactionFilter` adapter in `src/plugin/`: outline-mode gate
      via `editorInfoField`, fact extraction (userEvent annotation, composition state,
      change spans, selection ranges), classify, pass through unmodified (Phase A:
      no transaction is altered)
- [ ] 2.3 Implement the stats surface: per-class counters, timing aggregates
      (median/p95), bounded ring buffer; expose as a public field on the plugin
      instance for e2e reads
- [ ] 2.4 Gate per-transaction console logging behind the existing crosscheck debug
      setting; add the dev command that prints a stats summary
- [ ] 2.5 Register the extension in `main.ts` alongside grammar/decorations; verify
      off-mode notes record nothing (spec: off-mode untouched)

## 3. Phase A evidence suite (choke-point assumptions, falsifiable)

- [ ] 3.1 New e2e spec: coverage matrix — typing, paste, mouse drag selection, keyboard
      selection, find-and-replace, drag-drop text, programmatic edit each assert their
      observed classification via the stats surface
- [ ] 3.2 e2e: programmatic/remote pass-through — `setValue`-style full-document
      replacement and undo/redo classified `programmatic`, applied byte-identically
- [ ] 3.3 e2e: plugin-own recognition — grammar Tab/Enter and structural commands
      classified `plugin-own`, output byte-identical to pre-filter behavior
- [ ] 3.4 e2e: nested-editor degeneracy — type/select/drag inside a wide-table cell;
      assert no enforcement effect and stock cell behavior (D6 primary hypothesis)
- [ ] 3.5 e2e: non-mutation contract — a scripted edit sequence yields a buffer
      byte-identical to the same sequence with the plugin's filter absent
- [ ] 3.6 Performance measurement on a ~2000-line stress note: record median/p95
      against the D7 budget; add the stress fixture if the corpus lacks one
- [ ] 3.7 IME composition check (manual if not automatable in the harness): composition
      transactions classified `composition` and uninterfered; record the verdict
- [ ] 3.8 Record Phase A findings (observed userEvent values per source, timings,
      nested-editor verdict) in design.md and docs/research/04; falsified assumptions
      block Phase B until the design is amended

## 4. Selection escalation core (Phase B foundation)

- [ ] 4.1 Implement escalation math as a pure function in `src/` per D4: same-node
      pass-through, deepest-common-ancestor sibling-subtree cover, orientation
      preservation, preamble jurisdiction (D5), per-range multi-selection handling
- [ ] 4.2 Unit + property tests: escalated ranges always start/end at subtree
      boundaries, idempotence (escalating an escalated range is a no-op), orientation
      preserved, within-node and cursor inputs unchanged, preamble ranges unchanged

## 5. Selection enforcement wiring (Phase B)

- [ ] 5.1 Extend the filter adapter: for `selection-only` transactions, apply escalation
      and return `[tr, { selection }]`; all other classes keep selections untouched
- [ ] 5.2 e2e: boundary-crossing mouse drag (forward and backward) escalates to whole
      subtrees; within-node drag and double-click word selection stay native
- [ ] 5.3 e2e: Shift+ArrowDown crossing a boundary escalates; heading→section subtree
      case; live-drag stability across multiple pointer updates (D4/risk scenario)
- [ ] 5.4 e2e: Select All with frontmatter is stock; off-mode drag is stock;
      programmatic mid-node selection restore is untouched
- [ ] 5.5 Multi-range escalation test (unit-level at minimum; e2e if the harness can
      produce multi-cursor selections)

## 6. Verification and closure

- [ ] 6.1 Full test suite (unit + all e2e specs) green twice consecutively; lint clean
- [ ] 6.2 Build and install into the dev vault (`npm run build:plugin && npm run
      vault:install`); real-vault manual pass focused on selection feel (escalation
      aggressiveness, drag stability) per the experiment-series discipline
- [ ] 6.3 Record the manual-pass verdict and the open-question answers (drag timing,
      escalate-vs-clamp feel, perf numbers) in design.md; park any deferred UX ideas
      in the decoration follow-ups doc or a new follow-ups home as appropriate

I'm reviewing the existing e2e test files to understand the patterns they use for selection and keyboard simulation, and checking if helpers.ts already provides a setSelection utility I can leverage.

For the mouse drag simulation coverage, I need to implement genuine pointer events (mousedown, mousemove, mouseup) using WebdriverIO's browser actions API rather than relying on the Obsidian API's setSelection, since the latter might not trigger CodeMirror's native pointer event handlers the same way real mouse interaction would. I'll need to compute click and drag coordinates using the existing getLineRect helper and target the `.cm-line` elements directly. Let me first check if any of the existing specs already have pointer or mouse-based interaction examples I can build from.

content.thinking:
I don't have an existing e2e test pattern for mouse dragging, so I'll need to implement that from scratch. I see there's already a test using `.click()` directly on a webdriverio element, but for drag operations I can use either the element's `dragAndDrop` method or the lower-level `browser.actions()` API with pointer sequences that webdriverio supports.

Let me check what webdriverio version is installed and what pointer action APIs are available. I'm leaning toward using the modern `browser.action('pointer')` builder approach with move, down, move, up, and perform calls — this is the standard W3C Actions API that webdriverio v8+ supports. I'll create a helper function that takes source and target rectangles and performs the drag sequence, deriving coordinates from the existing line rect utilities or simpler click-and-drag logic.

The tricky part is handling pixel-perfect character positioning in the actual running Obsidian CM6 editor, which requires computing exact X coordinates for text selection.  Instead of trying to calculate per-character offsets, I'll use a more robust approach: click near the start of a line (left edge plus small offset) and drag to the end of another line (using the full width or even overshooting horizontally, since CM6 clamps to end-of-line content). This avoids needing precise character-width math and should work reliably across different text widths.

For the actual drag implementation, I'll use pointer-based actions with mousedown at the start position and mouseup at the end, moving through intermediate points. While a simple three-step drag (down→move→up) might only generate one final selection transaction, adding 3-4 intermediate move steps with pauses will produce separate mousemove events that each trigger CM6 selection-extending transactions—this is especially important for the stability test that verifies live dragging across multiple pointer updates.

The main blocker now is that I can't actually verify any of this against a real running Obsidian instance without executing the e2e suite, which requires downloading Obsidian binaries.

Now I'm adding helper functions to the test utilities—one to capture the plugin stats snapshot and another to reset them, plus a drag selection helper that takes line and character coordinates.

For the drag selection, I'll use CodeMirror 6's `coordsAtPos` API to get precise pixel coordinates for any document position, which is much more reliable than approximating from line measurements. 1	import type { BaseActionParams } from '../../utils/actions/index.js';
2	import { KeyAction, PointerAction, WheelAction } from '../../utils/actions/index.js';
3	/**
4	 * The action command is a low-level interface for providing virtualized device input actions to the web browser.
5	 *
6	 * In addition to high level commands such like `scrollIntoView`, `doubleClick`, the Actions API provides granular
7	 * control over exactly what designated input devices can do. WebdriverIO provides an interface for 3 kinds of input
8	 * sources:
9	 *
10	 * - a key input for keyboard devices
11	 * - a pointer input for a mouse, pen or touch devices
12	 * - and wheel inputs for scroll wheel devices
13	 *
14	 * Every chain of action commands has to be completed calling `perform` in order to trigger the set of actions. This
15	 * causes actions [to be released](https://w3c.github.io/webdriver/#release-actions) and events to be fired. You can
16	 * skip this by passing in `true` (e.g. `browser.actions(...).perform(true)`).
17	 *
18	 * :::info
19	 *
20	 * Support for this command and specific actions can differ based on the environment. Progress on the development
21	 * can be followed on [wpt.fyi](https://wpt.fyi/results/webdriver/tests/perform_actions?label=experimental&label=master&aligned).
22	 * For mobile you might want to use Appium specific gesture commands on [iOS](https://appium.github.io/appium-xcuitest-driver/latest/guides/gestures/)
23	 * and [Android](https://github.com/appium/appium-uiautomator2-driver#mobile-gesture-commands).
24	 *
25	 * :::
26	 *
27	 * ### Key input source
28	 *
29	 * A key input source is an input source that is associated with a keyboard-type device. It can be triggered
30	 * by using the `key` type parameters. e.g.:
31	 *
32	 * ```ts
33	 * browser.action('key')
34	 * ```
35	 *
36	 * It returns a `KeyAction` object that supports the following actions:
37	 *
38	 * - `down(value: string)`: generates a key down action
39	 * - `up(value: string)`: generates a key up action
40	 * - `pause(ms: number)`: indicate that an input source does nothing during a particular tick
41	 *
42	 * #### Special Characters
43	 *
44	 * If you like to use special characters like e.g. `Control`, `Page Up` or `Shift`, make sure to import the
45	 * [`Key`](/docs/api/modules#key) object from the `webdriverio` package like so:
46	 *
47	 * ```ts
48	 * import { Key } from 'webdriverio'
49	 * ```
50	 *
51	 * The `Key` object provides constants for all special keys including `Key.Ctrl` (cross-platform), `Key.Enter`,
52	 * `Key.Tab`, `Key.ArrowLeft`, arrow keys, function keys, and more. See the [Key API docs](/docs/api/modules#key)
53	 * for a complete list.
54	 *
55	 * ### Pointer input source
56	 *
57	 * A pointer input source is an input source that is associated with a pointer-type input device. The type can be
58	 * specified when invoking the `action` command, e.g.:
59	 *
60	 * ```ts
61	 * browser.action('pointer', {
62	 *     parameters: { pointerType: 'mouse' } // "mouse" is default value, also possible: "pen" or "touch"
63	 * })
64	 * ```
65	 *
66	 * It returns a `PointerAction` object that supports the following actions:
67	 *
68	 * - `down (button: 'left' | 'middle' | 'right')`: creates an action to press a single key
69	 * - `down (params: PointerActionParams)`: creates an action to press a single key with detailed parameters
70	 * - `move (x: number, y: number)`: Creates an action for moving the pointer `x` and `y` pixels from the viewport
71	 * - `move (params: PointerActionMoveParams)`: Creates an action for moving the pointer `x` and `y` pixels from the
72	 *   specified `origin`. The `origin` may be defined as the pointers current position (e.g. "pointer"), the viewport
73	 *   (e.g. "viewport") or the center of a specific element.
74	 * - `up (button: 'left' | 'middle' | 'right')`: creates an action to release a single key
75	 * - `up (params: PointerActionUpParams)`: creates an action to release a single key with detailed parameters
76	 * - `cancel()`: An action that cancels this pointer's current input.
77	 * - `pause(ms: number)`: indicate that an input source does nothing during a particular tick
78	 *
79	 * You can find detailed information on the [`PointerActionParams`](https://github.com/webdriverio/webdriverio/blob/8ca026c75bf7c27ef9d574f0ec48d8bc13658602/packages/webdriverio/src/utils/actions/pointer.ts#L20-L35), [`PointerActionMoveParams`](https://github.com/webdriverio/webdriverio/blob/8ca026c75bf7c27ef9d574f0ec48d8bc13658602/packages/webdriverio/src/utils/actions/pointer.ts#L20-L42) and [`PointerActionUpParams`](https://github.com/webdriverio/webdriverio/blob/8ca026c75bf7c27ef9d574f0ec48d8bc13658602/packages/webdriverio/src/utils/actions/pointer.ts#L13-L19)
80	 * parameter types in the project type definition.
81	 *
82	 * ### Wheel input source
83	 *
84	 * A wheel input source is an input source that is associated with a wheel-type input device.
85	 *
86	 * ```ts
87	 * browser.action('wheel')
88	 * ```
89	 *
90	 * It returns a `WheelAction` object that supports the following actions:
91	 *
92	 * - `scroll (params: ScrollParams)`: scrolls a page to given coordinates or origin
93	 * - `pause(ms: number)`: indicate that an input source does nothing during a particular tick
94	 *
95	 * You can find detailed information on the [`ScrollParams`](https://github.com/webdriverio/webdriverio/blob/8ca026c75bf7c27ef9d574f0ec48d8bc13658602/packages/webdriverio/src/utils/actions/wheel.ts#L4-L29) parameter type in the project type definition.
96	 *
97	 * <example>
98	    :pointer-action.js
99	    it('drag and drop using pointer action command', async () => {
100	        const origin = await $('#source')
101	        const targetOrigin = await $('#target')
102	
103	        return browser.action('pointer')
104	            .move({ duration: 0, origin, x: 0, y: 0 })
105	            .down({ button: 0 }) // left button
106	            .pause(10)
107	            .move({ duration: 0, origin: targetOrigin })
108	            .up({ button: 0 })
109	            .perform()
110	    });
111	    :key-action.js
112	    import { Key } from 'webdriverio'
113	
114	    it('should emit key events using key action commands', async () => {
115	        const elem = await $('input')
116	        await elem.click() // make element active
117	
118	        await browser.action('key')
119	            .down('f')
120	            .down('o')
121	            .down('o')
122	            .up('f')
123	            .up('o')
124	            .up('o')
125	            .perform()
126	
127	        console.log(await elem.getValue()) // returns "foo"
128	
129	        // copy value out of input element
130	        await browser.action('key')
131	            .down(Key.Ctrl).down('c')
132	            .pause(10)
133	            .up(Key.Ctrl).up('c')
134	            .perform()
135	    })
136	    :wheel-action.js
137	    it('should scroll using wheel action commands', async () => {
138	        console.log(await browser.execute(() => window.scrollY)) // returns 0
139	        await browser.action('wheel').scroll({
140	            deltaX: 0,
141	            deltaY: 500,
142	            duration: 200
143	        }).perform()
144	        console.log(await browser.execute(() => window.scrollY)) // returns 500
145	    })
146	 * </example>
147	 *
148	 * @alias browser.action
149	 * @type utility
150	 *
151	 */
152	export declare function action(this: WebdriverIO.Browser, type: 'key', opts?: Pick<BaseActionParams, 'id'>): KeyAction;
153	export declare function action(this: WebdriverIO.Browser, type: 'pointer', opts?: BaseActionParams): PointerAction;
154	export declare function action(this: WebdriverIO.Browser, type: 'wheel', opts?: Pick<BaseActionParams, 'id'>): WheelAction;
155	//# sourceMappingURL=action.d.ts.map

import type { BaseActionParams } from '../../utils/actions/index.js';
import { KeyAction, PointerAction, WheelAction } from '../../utils/actions/index.js';
/**
 * The action command is a low-level interface for providing virtualized device input actions to the web browser.
 *
 * In addition to high level commands such like `scrollIntoView`, `doubleClick`, the Actions API provides granular
 * control over exactly what designated input devices can do. WebdriverIO provides an interface for 3 kinds of input
 * sources:
 *
 * - a key input for keyboard devices
 * - a pointer input for a mouse, pen or touch devices
 * - and wheel inputs for scroll wheel devices
 *
 * Every chain of action commands has to be completed calling `perform` in order to trigger the set of actions. This
 * causes actions [to be released](https://w3c.github.io/webdriver/#release-actions) and events to be fired. You can
 * skip this by passing in `true` (e.g. `browser.actions(...).perform(true)`).
 *
 * :::info
 *
 * Support for this command and specific actions can differ based on the environment. Progress on the development
 * can be followed on [wpt.fyi](https://wpt.fyi/results/webdriver/tests/perform_actions?label=experimental&label=master&aligned).
 * For mobile you might want to use Appium specific gesture commands on [iOS](https://appium.github.io/appium-xcuitest-driver/latest/guides/gestures/)
 * and [Android](https://github.com/appium/appium-uiautomator2-driver#mobile-gesture-commands).
 *
 * :::
 *
 * ### Key input source
 *
 * A key input source is an input source that is associated with a keyboard-type device. It can be triggered
 * by using the `key` type parameters. e.g.:
 *
 * ```ts
 * browser.action('key')
 * ```
 *
 * It returns a `KeyAction` object that supports the following actions:
 *
 * - `down(value: string)`: generates a key down action
 * - `up(value: string)`: generates a key up action
 * - `pause(ms: number)`: indicate that an input source does nothing during a particular tick
 *
 * #### Special Characters
 *
 * If you like to use special characters like e.g. `Control`, `Page Up` or `Shift`, make sure to import the
 * [`Key`](/docs/api/modules#key) object from the `webdriverio` package like so:
 *
 * ```ts
 * import { Key } from 'webdriverio'
 * ```
 *
 * The `Key` object provides constants for all special keys including `Key.Ctrl` (cross-platform), `Key.Enter`,
 * `Key.Tab`, `Key.ArrowLeft`, arrow keys, function keys, and more. See the [Key API docs](/docs/api/modules#key)
 * for a complete list.
 *
 * ### Pointer input source
 *
 * A pointer input source is an input source that is associated with a pointer-type input device. The type can be
 * specified when invoking the `action` command, e.g.:
 *
 * ```ts
 * browser.action('pointer', {
 *     parameters: { pointerType: 'mouse' } // "mouse" is default value, also possible: "pen" or "touch"
 * })
 * ```
 *
 * It returns a `PointerAction` object that supports the following actions:
 *
 * - `down (button: 'left' | 'middle' | 'right')`: creates an action to press a single key
 * - `down (params: PointerActionParams)`: creates an action to press a single key with detailed parameters
 * - `move (x: number, y: number)`: Creates an action for moving the pointer `x` and `y` pixels from the viewport
 * - `move (params: PointerActionMoveParams)`: Creates an action for moving the pointer `x` and `y` pixels from the
 *   specified `origin`. The `origin` may be defined as the pointers current position (e.g. "pointer"), the viewport
 *   (e.g. "viewport") or the center of a specific element.
 * - `up (button: 'left' | 'middle' | 'right')`: creates an action to release a single key
 * - `up (params: PointerActionUpParams)`: creates an action to release a single key with detailed parameters
 * - `cancel()`: An action that cancels this pointer's current input.
 * - `pause(ms: number)`: indicate that an input source does nothing during a particular tick
 *
 * You can find detailed information on the [`PointerActionParams`](https://github.com/webdriverio/webdriverio/blob/8ca026c75bf7c27ef9d574f0ec48d8bc13658602/packages/webdriverio/src/utils/actions/pointer.ts#L20-L35), [`PointerActionMoveParams`](https://github.com/webdriverio/webdriverio/blob/8ca026c75bf7c27ef9d574f0ec48d8bc13658602/packages/webdriverio/src/utils/actions/pointer.ts#L20-L42) and [`PointerActionUpParams`](https://github.com/webdriverio/webdriverio/blob/8ca026c75bf7c27ef9d574f0ec48d8bc13658602/packages/webdriverio/src/utils/actions/pointer.ts#L13-L19)
 * parameter types in the project type definition.
 *
 * ### Wheel input source
 *
 * A wheel input source is an input source that is associated with a wheel-type input device.
 *
 * ```ts
 * browser.action('wheel')
 * ```
 *
 * It returns a `WheelAction` object that supports the following actions:
 *
 * - `scroll (params: ScrollParams)`: scrolls a page to given coordinates or origin
 * - `pause(ms: number)`: indicate that an input source does nothing during a particular tick
 *
 * You can find detailed information on the [`ScrollParams`](https://github.com/webdriverio/webdriverio/blob/8ca026c75bf7c27ef9d574f0ec48d8bc13658602/packages/webdriverio/src/utils/actions/wheel.ts#L4-L29) parameter type in the project type definition.
 *
 * <example>
    :pointer-action.js
    it('drag and drop using pointer action command', async () => {
        const origin = await $('#source')
        const targetOrigin = await $('#target')

        return browser.action('pointer')
            .move({ duration: 0, origin, x: 0, y: 0 })
            .down({ button: 0 }) // left button
            .pause(10)
            .move({ duration: 0, origin: targetOrigin })
            .up({ button: 0 })
            .perform()
    });
    :key-action.js
    import { Key } from 'webdriverio'

    it('should emit key events using key action commands', async () => {
        const elem = await $('input')
        await elem.click() // make element active

        await browser.action('key')
            .down('f')
            .down('o')
            .down('o')
            .up('f')
            .up('o')
            .up('o')
            .perform()

        console.log(await elem.getValue()) // returns "foo"

        // copy value out of input element
        await browser.action('key')
            .down(Key.Ctrl).down('c')
            .pause(10)
            .up(Key.Ctrl).up('c')
            .perform()
    })
    :wheel-action.js
    it('should scroll using wheel action commands', async () => {
        console.log(await browser.execute(() => window.scrollY)) // returns 0
        await browser.action('wheel').scroll({
            deltaX: 0,
            deltaY: 500,
            duration: 200
        }).perform()
        console.log(await browser.execute(() => window.scrollY)) // returns 500
    })
 * </example>
 *
 * @alias browser.action
 * @type utility
 *
 */
export declare function action(this: WebdriverIO.Browser, type: 'key', opts?: Pick<BaseActionParams, 'id'>): KeyAction;
export declare function action(this: WebdriverIO.Browser, type: 'pointer', opts?: BaseActionParams): PointerAction;
export declare function action(this: WebdriverIO.Browser, type: 'wheel', opts?: Pick<BaseActionParams, 'id'>): WheelAction;
//# sourceMappingURL=action.d.ts.map

content.thinking:
I'll pass coordinates explicitly as `{x, y, origin: 'viewport'}` to match the documented semantics, since `coordsAtPos` returns viewport-relative client coordinates that align directly with how the viewport origin interprets absolute pixel positions. Now I'm designing the next part.

I'm writing helper functions to convert line and character positions into viewport coordinates, then using those to simulate a mouse drag selection across the editor by calculating intermediate points between the start and end positions. Writing helper functions... Adding helper for external file edits... The drag-and-drop text scenario is tricky because WebdriverIO's pointer actions don't reliably trigger the native HTML5 drag-and-drop events that CodeMirror 6 expects — I'd need actual OS-level drag machinery rather than just simulated pointer movements to properly test that interaction.

I'll implement a best-effort approach using pointer actions to simulate dragging selected text, but I'm flagging this as something I'll need to validate by running the suite and potentially falling back to keyboard-based alternatives if the drag-drop truly can't be synthesized reliably.

Now I'm ready to write out the two e2e spec files.

+ * editor) would: through `Vault.process`, which Obsidian diffs into any
+ * currently-open editor for that file as a no-userEvent transaction — the
+ * real-world "programmatic/remote" path, not just `setValue`. */

+/** Viewport pixel coordinates of a document position, via CM6's own

+export function posToCoords(

+    ({ app, obsidian }, line, ch) => {

+      if (!view) throw new Error('no active markdown view');
+      const cm = (view.editor as any).cm;

+      const coords = cm.coordsAtPos(pos);

+      return { left: coords.left, top: coords.top, bottom: coords.bottom };

+ * Real mouse drag selection: a genuine W3C pointer down/move.../up sequence

+ * userEvent path a real user's drag produces — the thing Phase A's
+ * choke-point claim ("every mutation path flows through the filter") is
+ * actually about. `steps` intermediate moves let a test assert the
+ * live-drag stability scenario (each pointer update stays escalated, no
+ * flicker), not just the final released position.
+export async function mouseDragSelect(
+  from: { line: number; ch: number },
+  to: { line: number; ch: number },
+  steps = 3,
+): Promise<void> {
+  const fromCoords = await posToCoords(from.line, from.ch);
+  const toCoords = await posToCoords(to.line, to.ch);
+  const fromY = Math.round((fromCoords.top + fromCoords.bottom) / 2);
+  const toY = Math.round((toCoords.top + toCoords.bottom) / 2);
+  const fromX = Math.round(fromCoords.left);
+  const toX = Math.round(toCoords.left);
+  const action = browser.action('pointer', { parameters: { pointerType: 'mouse' } });
+  action.move({ x: fromX, y: fromY, origin: 'viewport' }).down({ button: 0 }).pause(20);
+  for (let i = 1; i <= steps; i++) {
+    const t = i / steps;
+    action.move({
+      x: Math.round(fromX + (toX - fromX) * t),
+      y: Math.round(fromY + (toY - fromY) * t),
+      origin: 'viewport',
+      duration: 30,
+  action.up({ button: 0 });

content.thinking:
Now I'm setting up the end-to-end test spec for transaction classification, covering all the interaction patterns from the task list—typing, pasting, mouse and keyboard selection, find-and-replace, drag-drop operations, and programmatic edits—each one verifying that the classification updates correctly in the stats. I'll also need to handle the programmatic and remote pass-through scenarios with setValue-style operations and undo/redo functionality.

For the plugin-own recognition test, I'm checking that Tab and Enter trigger the correct classification and that the output matches the pre-filter behavior exactly, since Phase A doesn't modify the core logic. I'll verify the stats reflect the plugin-own classification and the buffer state matches what the existing keyboard grammar tests already assert. Then I'm testing nested-editor degeneracy by typing and selecting inside wide-table cells to ensure the filter doesn't interfere with normal cell behavior, and finally I'm approximating the non-mutation contract test by confirming that classification and logging operations don't alter the buffer, cursor position, or undo stack—similar to how the decoration contracts test validates this boundary. So the test strategy is to run the same edit sequence in outline mode versus off-mode and verify the final buffer is byte-identical, which confirms the filter isn't corrupting text for pass-through edits. I'll also check that selection-only changes don't mutate text (which they shouldn't by definition) and verify the undo stack stays clean for boundary-crossing edits, mirroring the non-mutation contracts from the decoration tests.

Then I need to measure performance on a larger stress note against the budget of 1ms median and 8ms p95, and handle IME composition testing as a manual verification step since automating it reliably through the test harness is tricky—I'll either skip it with documentation or attempt a best-effort approach using the Chrome DevTools Protocol.

For the wide-table fixture, I should check if there's an existing markdown table in the decorations fixture file that I can reuse, or just construct a simple inline table similar to what the decoration contracts test uses. I'll also need to look at the package.json test script to see how the e2e tests are invoked.

The next test file should be numbered 60 or 61 since 53 is already taken. For the paste simulation, WebdriverIO doesn't have reliable cross-platform clipboard support in Electron, so I need to use a genuine ctrl+v paste event rather than programmatic selection replacement—this means leveraging the Clipboard API through `navigator.clipboard.writeText()` in the Electron context to ensure CM6 receives the actual `input.paste` user event.

Writing the paste helper...

For find-and-replace, Obsidian's built-in panel opens with Cmd+H or via commands like 'editor:open-search' and 'editor:open-search-replace', but interacting with the DOM inputs and buttons would be fragile. I'm considering whether Obsidian has a direct command like 'editor:replace-selection' that could handle this more reliably. I'll use the documented `editor:open-search` command to trigger the find panel, then interact with the DOM to access the replace toggle and inputs, similar to how other interactions are already handled in the test suite. Given the time constraints and potential fragility, I'm implementing this as a best-effort approach and will simplify if the live run reveals issues. Now I'm ready to write out the full spec files with all these implementations and run the suite.

Now I'm moving on to write the Phase B end-to-end selection-enforcement test suite, making sure to document the IME task as a manual-only note somewhere in the codebase or design docs. I'll create the test file that covers boundary-crossing mouse drags in both directions escalating to whole subtrees, along with within-node drag and double-click word selection scenarios.

For the keyboard-based tests, I'm handling Shift+ArrowDown crossing boundaries to escalate selections, testing heading-to-section subtree cases, and ensuring live-drag stability across multiple pointer updates. I'll also verify that Select All with frontmatter works as expected, off-mode drag remains stock behavior, and programmatic mid-node selection restoration stays untouched. Multi-range escalation will be tested at the unit level, with e2e coverage if the test harness can produce multi-cursor selections.

For the implementation, I'm using helper methods like mouseDragSelect, getSelection, setSelection, setCursor, and isOutlineMode to drive the tests. For double-click word selection, I need to simulate a double-click at specific pixel coordinates using WebdriverIO's pointer actions—either through two closely-spaced click cycles or by finding a way to invoke the element's doubleClick method on exact coordinates.

Adding helper functions to handle double-click at line/character position... I'm deciding between unit testing the multi-range iteration logic in isolation or doing an end-to-end test with the actual editor, and leaning toward a focused adapter-level assertion since the core escalation logic per range is already covered by property tests, even though testing multiple ranges together would require either mocking CodeMirror or using the full harness. I need to check whether WebdriverIO's pointer actions support modifier keys directly, and if not, I'll need to use the W3C Actions API approach where keyboard and pointer inputs run in parallel across synchronized ticks—though combining multiple action chains in WebdriverIO requires careful sequencing before calling `.perform()`.

Since the task explicitly allows unit-level testing as sufficient, I'll focus on adding a targeted unit test rather than getting bogged down in the e2e modifier-key complexity. I should verify whether Obsidian's default setup actually includes the "select next occurrence" command that VSCode has, since that's not guaranteed in CodeMirror by default — and given that uncertainty, I might need to skip the e2e test and just document the unit-level coverage in the task notes instead. Ctrl+Click is the standard multi-cursor binding in most editors, so I'll use WebdriverIO's action chaining to simulate holding a modifier key while clicking — the key insight is that each `perform()` call sends an isolated action and releases state by default, so I need to keep the modifier key held across multiple pointer events by chaining them in a single action sequence before calling `perform()`. Writing the test pattern...

The approach relies on W3C actions spec behavior where input state persists across separate `performActions` calls within a session until explicitly released, so holding Ctrl while performing a pointer drag should work. I'll implement the multi-range selection test using this pattern and validate it during the actual e2e run—if it fails I can simplify, but given this is lower priority I'd rather test it live than overthink it further.
