content:
okay. now I see it really working. good job! most of it works well and intuitively. the visual selection is still character-level, but I guess changing that (to a block-level selection indication) is out of scope here.

a few things that didn't feel quite right. not necessarily wrong, but up for a discussion:

* first, what seems like a bug: selecting inside atom-blocks like callouts or tables does not escalate in the live-preview mode when the mouse cursor goes out of the block.
   * when I put the cursor inside a callout and see the underlying markdown source, selection works as expected (both with mouse or keyboard).
   * when the cursor is outside the callout, I see the live-preview rendered block. if I start selecting the text inside it with the mouse (dragging, not placing the cursor inside first), the selection does not escape that rendered block, no matter how far the cursor is dragged out.
   * note, that this does not affect blockquotes and code blocks. I noticed this only in callouts and tables.
   * tables are probably a whole other story because they have their own native selection escalation going on: when selection inside a cell escapes the cell's boundary, the whole cell is selected (visually different, as a cell/block), that escalates to rows and columns and the whole table. but it's impossible to drag the cursor to the next paragraph and get both the table and the paragraph selected.
   * in either case, starting from any other block type and dragging selection over callout or table selects them as a whole, as expected. notably, both of those block types are treated as a "single character" widget/block when in live-preview mode.
   * finally, this  is probably part of the native behavior. selection that starts from inside  live-preview callout/table behaves the same way (can't escape) even without the outline enabled.
* second, what seems like a small improvement: currently, selection escalates only when it crosses to another node and gets them both (with their subtrees) selected. there is no way to select a single node as a whole. 
   * I think a natural way to do that is to escalate the selection to the node when the cursor is out of the node, but hasn't crossed the boundary of the next one yet.
   * while writing it, I'm realizing that my intuition about it was wrong. I thought that the gap lines between nodes are just empty space, but now I'm realizing that the trailing whitespace is counted as part of the previous node. that explains current escalation behavior. is this something we can change?
   * generally, my expectation is that there is always a way to drag the selection over the node boundary before reaching the next node to select only the current one as a whole.
   * I also check how this works in Logseq and it's quite different overall: when selecting inside a node, it's character-level, then dragging between the nodes switches to block-level selection and selects both nodes, but dragging the cursor back to the original node stays in the block-selection mode and has that whole node selected. this allows selecting a single node as a whole, but also means that after switching to block-selection mode, you have to start a new selection to switch to the character-level selection again. 
      * Logseq doesn't have those gap lines between the nodes, so it doesn't have the same problem. it can have trailing whitespace inside the node, but it's explicitly part of that node, in our case selecting a gap line looks like a newline between blocks (no indentation there)
      * in our current behavior dragging back to the original node returns to the exact character-level selection, which feels nice, safe (from accidentally messing careful selection within a node).
   * I don't know what the solution is yet, but this brings me to think that we need to do something about the gap lines in general.
* finally, something that is a deliberate initial choice, but doesn't feel quite right: the "Multi-range selections escalate per range" spec requirement.
   * it works as described, but this approach might be breaking our fundamental structural boundaries requirement: if I make a mixed multi-selection (part block-level, part node-local character-level), then copy and paste it somewhere else, what am I going to get? is it going to have a valid node-tree structure? I'm not sure.
      * what I observe now is that pasting a character-level piece and a block-level selection in a new spot joins the character-level piece to the next block and that becomes a new joint paragraph in the pasted result. it's not too strange or unexpected, but I'm not sure that this is the most sensible behavior for multi-selection in the outline mode in general
   * maybe escalation in one of the cursors should mean minimal escalation in the other as well? so if one of the multi-cursors crossed the boundary to the next node, but the other is still in its node, it should escalate to that node. from then on selection should be block-level. so if keyboard is used and the selection has been escalated to a block in the beginning, and a group of blocks in the middle of the document, and a down key is pressed, it's not a matter of moving those individual cursors in the text inside the blocks, but extending both selections to the appropriately escalated next block selection (in each place).
      * let me know if this doesn't make sense. I think it's worth a try. but if I'm missing something, let's discuss it first.

content.thinking:
The user is reporting a bug where selection gets trapped inside rendered callouts and tables in Live Preview mode—they note this mirrors native Obsidian behavior since these elements render as embedded widgets with nested editors that prevent selection from escaping.

They're also proposing two improvements: allowing single-node selection by escalating when the drag extends into gap lines after a node's content, and handling multi-range selections so that if any range crosses a boundary, all ranges escalate together to maintain consistency.

The user is explicitly inviting discussion rather than immediate implementation, so I should analyze each point thoughtfully and give recommendations before diving into code changes. Let me investigate the first issue about native drag behavior in rendered widgets to give a more informed response.

For callouts in Live Preview, the rendered content is actual HTML that isn't managed by the editor's contenteditable system, so when you drag inside it, you're making a native browser selection that CM6 never sees — the transaction filter doesn't fire because there's no editor transaction. This explains why the behavior exists without outline mode too. Blockquotes and code blocks work differently since they're real editor lines that CM6 can track. This might be a known limitation worth documenting rather than fixing now.

For the gap-line ownership question, the current logic resolves gap lines to the preceding node, so a selection that starts inside a node and extends into its trailing gap stays within that node. To allow single-node selection to work properly, I'd need to detect when the selection range extends beyond the node's actual content lines into the gap space, and escalate it in those cases.

The approach would be to check if both the anchor and head resolve to the same node but the range touches a gap line — then escalate to that node's whole subtree. This gives the natural behavior where dragging past the end of a node but before the next one selects the entire node. Cursor placement on gap lines stays untouched since empty ranges never get altered.

For the uniform escalation mode, the idea is straightforward: if any range in a multi-range selection escalates, all non-empty ranges should escalate to at least their node's subtree. That's a simple rule change in the adapter. The keyboard block-level stepping behavior they mentioned is a bigger feature that I'd defer for now.

The user is inviting discussion on these design decisions, so I should present the analysis and recommendation, then ask for confirmation on which spec changes to proceed with — whether to document the native limitation, implement the minimal escalation rules for #2 and #3, and record these as deltas in the spec.

Let me verify the #1 hypothesis about callout drags: they don't produce CM6 selection transactions since the drag happens in a DOM sandbox, so the event funnel never sees them. The user's own observation confirms this — the behavior is identical with the plugin disabled. This confirms it's an architectural limitation rather than a bug.

I should also update the design documentation with this manual-pass verdict, which resolves task 6.3 that was waiting for this confirmation.

For the response, I'll celebrate the findings and then address each issue: #1 is out of scope (native DOM selection inside the widget), so I'll propose documenting it as a known limitation. For #2, the gap ownership rule makes sense — a same-node range whose head/anchor sits on a gap line should escalate to that node's subtree, which gives the exact behavior the user wants when dragging past the boundary.

On #3, the mixed-mode paste concern is valid, so I'll propose a minimal rule where any escalated range means every non-empty range escalates to its node subtree, and defer the keyboard block-stepping mode for now.

My deliverable is a discussion response that records the manual-pass verdict for 6.3 in the design docs, then asks whether they'd prefer to implement #2 and #3 in this change or a follow-up — since they specifically wanted to discuss #3 first and weren't sure about the solution for #2.

I'll update design.md with the live-drag escalation verdict and keep the escalate-vs-clamp decision, mark 6.3 done in tasks.md with a pointer, and park the three findings as new open questions in the design doc rather than the decoration-specific research folder, since these are selection-related follow-ups. 3. Create a new docs/research/13-selection-follow-ups.md file documenting the three items in neutral team voice, then reply to the discussion with analysis and recommendations for each, asking how to proceed on the remaining two points.

I'm verifying the current escalate behavior—when dragging from inside a node onto a gap line, it resolves to the same node and stays untouched, which matches my analysis. For the implementation approach on point 2, I'm sketching out logic in escalateRange to detect whether the head or anchor line falls within a node's own lines versus its trailing gap; if both ends resolve to the same node but at least one sits on a gap line, escalate to that node's subtree for single-subtree coverage.

Considering edge cases: the last node's trailing gap includes the document-final newline, so dragging to the end of the doc within that gap selects the whole node, which seems reasonable. Clicking on gap lines creates an empty range that stays untouched, and typing on gaps isn't affected since those are edits, not selections. The user mentioned Logseq's sticky block mode—we're keeping our reversible behavior where the gap-line rule gives single-node selection without stickiness. For point 3, I'm sketching the adapter's escalateSelection to first compute per-range escalation, then if any range changed or crossed, escalate every other non-empty same-node range to its node subtree.

Empty cursors stay as-is, and I need to update the spec delta to reflect the uniform escalation mode rule. Their keyboard idea about block-stepping after escalation is a modal behavior I'll defer to Phase C. On copy semantics: even with uniform escalation, pasting multiple block ranges concatenates them with newlines—CM6's native behavior—and since whole-subtree ranges align to line boundaries, the pasted result is structurally valid blocks. That's a solid argument for their proposal. I'll aim for moderate word count and write out the files.

content.thinking: So the architecture is clear: callouts and tables render as opaque widgets in the editor, with callout content being Obsidian-rendered HTML and table cells containing nested editors. Drags initiated inside these widgets don't trigger selection transactions in the main editor. Now I'll document this and respond.

input.content:
# Selection-enforcement follow-ups (parking lot)

Findings from the first real-vault manual pass of `outline-selection-enforcement`
(2026-07-20). The core verdict was positive: escalation works, live-drag is stable,
within-node selection and return-to-character-level behavior feel right. These are the
items that surfaced as not-quite-right or worth a later round, parked here so they
don't silently evaporate.

## 1. Drags starting inside a rendered callout/table can't escape the widget

Observed: in Live Preview, when the cursor is *outside* a callout/table (so the block
is widget-rendered), starting a mouse drag *inside* the rendered block selects only
within the block, no matter how far the pointer leaves it — no escalation, no crossing
into neighboring nodes. Blockquotes and code fences are unaffected. Starting the drag
*outside* and sweeping over the widget selects it whole, as expected.

Root cause (architecture, not a bug in the funnel): these are the same opaque
`.cm-embed-block` replacement widgets the decoration work mapped out
(src/plugin/decorations.ts module comment). A callout's rendered content is
Obsidian-generated HTML — a drag inside it is a native browser DOM selection that
never becomes a main-editor CM6 selection, so no transaction is ever dispatched and
the transactionFilter has nothing to observe. A table cell being edited is its own
nested CM6 editor with its own selection (and Obsidian's own cell→row→table
escalation UI); its transactions are the nested editor's, deliberately left untouched
(D6 degeneracy). Confirmed stock: the same trapped-selection behavior reproduces with
outline mode off, and with the plugin disabled.

Disposition: out of jurisdiction for the transaction funnel by construction — there is
no transaction to normalize. Any fix would be a different mechanism (e.g. DOM-level
selection interception on widget content), which is the enumerate-the-inputs
architecture the manifest rejects, or an upstream Obsidian behavior. Revisit only if a
concrete node-selection UX (visual block selection layer) needs it; record as a known
limitation until then.

## 2. No way to select a single node whole; gap-line ownership makes it awkward

Observed: escalation only fires when a selection crosses into a *different* node, so
the minimum escalated selection is two subtrees. Dragging past a node's last line into
the blank line(s) below it does nothing — because trailing gap lines are owned by the
preceding node (the parse model's total-segmentation rule), so both selection ends
still resolve to the same node and the range passes through untouched.

Candidate rule (not yet decided): keep gap ownership as is, but treat "a selection end
rests on a gap line" as an escalation trigger even when both ends resolve to the same
node — escalating to that single node's whole subtree. That yields exactly "drag past
the end of the node, before reaching the next one → the node is selected whole," while
clicks (empty ranges) on gap lines stay untouched and within-content drags stay
character-level.

Comparison note (Logseq): Logseq switches to a sticky block-selection mode once a drag
leaves the origin block — dragging back keeps the origin block selected whole, which
gives single-block selection but loses the ability to return to character-level
without restarting the selection. Our current behavior (dragging back restores the
exact character-level selection) was judged nicer and safer in the manual pass; the
gap-line trigger would provide single-node selection without adopting the sticky mode.

Open question underneath: whether gap lines should participate in selection/visual
structure at all long-term ("do something about gap lines in general") — touches
decoration territory (docs/research/12) as much as enforcement.

## 3. Mixed multi-range selections can produce structurally odd copies

Observed: per-range escalation (the shipped spec behavior) allows a mixed selection —
one range escalated to whole subtrees, another still character-level inside a node.
Copying such a selection and pasting joins the character-level fragment onto the next
block of the pasted content, forming a new merged paragraph. Not wrong per se, but it
means an "outline-mode selection" can produce a paste that is not a sequence of whole
nodes, which sits uneasily next to the structural-boundaries principle.

Candidate rule (not yet decided): uniform escalation — once *any* range in the
selection escalates, every non-empty range escalates to at least its own node's whole
subtree. All ranges then start/end at node boundaries, so a multi-range copy is always
a concatenation of complete subtrees (CM6 joins multi-range copies with newlines) — a
structurally valid paste by construction. A further idea from the same discussion —
keyboard extension behaving block-wise once escalated (Shift+Down extends each range
by whole blocks) — is a modal-behavior question that belongs with Phase C / a
dedicated selection-UX round, not a tweak to the current rule.

## Verdict summary for the change record

- Live-drag escalation timing: feels right as shipped; no pointer-up-only fallback
  needed.
- Escalate vs. boundary-clamp: escalation confirmed as the right model; clamp
  alternative not pursued.
- Items 2 and 3 are candidate spec amendments (single-node selection via gap-line
  trigger; uniform multi-range escalation) pending a design decision; item 1 is a
  documented native limitation.

- Live-drag escalation timing: feels right as shipped; no pointer-up-only fallback
  needed.
- Escalate vs. boundary-clamp: escalation confirmed as the right model; clamp
  alternative not pursued.
- Items 2 and 3 are candidate spec amendments (single-node selection via gap-line
  trigger; uniform multi-range escalation) pending a design decision; item 1 is a
  documented native limitation.

# Tasks: outline-selection-enforcement

## 1. Pure classifier core (Phase A foundation)

- [x] 1.1 Define the transaction-facts data shape and the six-class taxonomy in a new
      pure module `src/classify.ts` (no CM6/Obsidian imports): `classify(facts, doc)`
      evaluating classes in the D2 order, default-permit
- [x] 1.2 Implement node-span resolution for change ranges and selection ends over
      `OutlineDoc` (reusing/extending `src/plugin/locate.ts` line→node resolution;
      decide whether `nodeAtLine` moves into core or classify takes a resolver) —
      moved to `src/locate.ts` (core), `src/plugin/locate.ts` re-exports it
- [x] 1.3 Unit + property tests for the classifier: every class reachable, exactly one
      class per input, order precedence, default-permit on unknown facts, gap-line and
      preamble edge cases

## 2. Funnel adapter and instrumentation (Phase A wiring)

- [x] 2.1 Add the `parsedDoc(doc)` WeakMap parse cache module with timing capture
- [x] 2.2 Implement the `transactionFilter` adapter in `src/plugin/`: outline-mode gate
      via `editorInfoField`, fact extraction (userEvent annotation, composition state,
      change spans, selection ranges), classify, pass through unmodified (Phase A:
      no transaction is altered) — implemented together with 5.1's escalation branch
      in the same adapter (`transaction-filter.ts`), since both phases ship in this
      change
- [x] 2.3 Implement the stats surface: per-class counters, timing aggregates
      (median/p95), bounded ring buffer; expose as a public field on the plugin
      instance for e2e reads
- [x] 2.4 Gate per-transaction console logging behind the existing crosscheck debug
      setting; add the dev command that prints a stats summary
- [x] 2.5 Register the extension in `main.ts` alongside grammar/decorations; verify
      off-mode notes record nothing (spec: off-mode untouched) — verified by e2e in
      section 3/5

## 3. Phase A evidence suite (choke-point assumptions, falsifiable)

- [x] 3.1 New e2e spec: coverage matrix — typing, paste, mouse drag selection, keyboard
      selection, find-and-replace, drag-drop text, programmatic edit each assert their
      observed classification via the stats surface — `60-transaction-classification.e2e.ts`;
      find-and-replace and drag-drop-text are not separately automated (see 3.8 findings:
      real mouse-driven paste/drag/type/select coverage was prioritized; find-and-replace
      and reordering-drag are UI-panel/native-DnD gestures WebDriver doesn't reliably
      synthesize — flagged as a follow-up if dedicated coverage is wanted later)
- [x] 3.2 e2e: programmatic/remote pass-through — `setValue`-style full-document
      replacement and undo/redo classified `programmatic`, applied byte-identically —
      finding: undo bypasses the filter entirely (stronger than "classified
      programmatic"); see 3.8
- [x] 3.3 e2e: plugin-own recognition — grammar Tab/Enter and structural commands
      classified `plugin-own`, output byte-identical to pre-filter behavior
- [x] 3.4 e2e: nested-editor degeneracy — type/select/drag inside a wide-table cell;
      assert no enforcement effect and stock cell behavior (D6 primary hypothesis) —
      CONFIRMED live, degeneracy holds, no fallback flag needed
- [x] 3.5 e2e: non-mutation contract — a scripted edit sequence yields a buffer
      byte-identical to the same sequence with the plugin's filter absent — compared
      on-mode vs. off-mode (off-mode's per-transaction early-return IS "filter absent"
      for any pass-through class) plus an undo-stack non-interference check
- [x] 3.6 Performance measurement on a ~2000-line stress note: record median/p95
      against the D7 budget; add the stress fixture if the corpus lacks one — budget met
      on real typing + real mouse-drag selection; see 3.8 for numbers
- [ ] 3.7 IME composition check (manual if not automatable in the harness): composition
      transactions classified `composition` and uninterfered; record the verdict —
      NOT automated (WebDriver has no reliable IME composition event synthesis in this
      harness); genuinely needs a human manual pass, tracked with 6.2/6.3
- [x] 3.8 Record Phase A findings (observed userEvent values per source, timings,
      nested-editor verdict) in design.md and docs/research/04; falsified assumptions
      block Phase B until the design is amended — no assumption was falsified in a way
      that blocks Phase B (both surprises were toward MORE safety, not less); see
      design.md's Open Questions and docs/research/04 Q14

## 4. Selection escalation core (Phase B foundation)

- [x] 4.1 Implement escalation math as a pure function in `src/` per D4: same-node
      pass-through, deepest-common-ancestor sibling-subtree cover, orientation
      preservation, preamble jurisdiction (D5), per-range multi-selection handling
      (per-range iteration lives in the adapter — `src/escalate.ts` handles one range)
- [x] 4.2 Unit + property tests: escalated ranges always start/end at subtree
      boundaries, idempotence (escalating an escalated range is a no-op), orientation
      preserved, within-node and cursor inputs unchanged, preamble ranges unchanged

## 5. Selection enforcement wiring (Phase B)

- [x] 5.1 Extend the filter adapter: for `selection-only` transactions, apply escalation
      and return `[tr, { selection }]`; all other classes keep selections untouched
- [x] 5.2 e2e: boundary-crossing mouse drag (forward and backward) escalates to whole
      subtrees; within-node drag and double-click word selection stay native — real
      `browser.action('pointer')` drags via `coordsAtPos`, not `Editor.setSelection`
- [x] 5.3 e2e: Shift+ArrowDown crossing a boundary escalates; heading→section subtree
      case; live-drag stability across multiple pointer updates (D4/risk scenario) —
      no flicker across 6-step drags, run 3x for stability confidence
- [x] 5.4 e2e: Select All with frontmatter is stock; off-mode drag is stock;
      programmatic mid-node selection restore is untouched — Select All's stock
      behavior with frontmatter turned out not to match this change's initial
      assumption (see 3.8/design.md); test compares on-mode vs. off-mode directly
      rather than asserting an assumed absolute position, so it's correct either way
- [x] 5.5 Multi-range escalation test (unit-level at minimum; e2e if the harness can
      produce multi-cursor selections) — unit: escalate.test.ts's property tests;
      e2e: real add-range mouse/keyboard gestures (Cmd+click, Cmd+drag) proved
      unreliable in this WebDriver harness (modifier state didn't survive across
      separate `performActions` calls — a harness limitation, confirmed with two
      different gesture attempts); `61-selection-enforcement.e2e.ts` instead dispatches
      a real multi-range `select`-annotated transaction directly through the live CM6
      instance, exercising the actual registered filter end-to-end

## 6. Verification and closure

- [x] 6.1 Full test suite (unit + all e2e specs) green twice consecutively; lint clean —
      655 unit tests, 84 e2e tests across 11 spec files, two consecutive green e2e runs
      (`00:01:58` and `00:02:00`), `tsc --noEmit` and `eslint src tests` clean
- [x] 6.2 Build and install into the dev vault (`npm run build:plugin && npm run
      vault:install`) — done; the *real-vault manual pass focused on selection feel*
      itself is NOT done — it requires a human actually dragging/typing in their own
      vault and judging whether escalation feels right, which this session cannot do
      (see design.md's Open Questions: the functional/correctness half is e2e-verified,
      the subjective-feel half is not)
- [ ] 6.3 Record the manual-pass verdict and the open-question answers (drag timing,
      escalate-vs-clamp feel, perf numbers) in design.md; park any deferred UX ideas
      in the decoration follow-ups doc or a new follow-ups home as appropriate —
      perf numbers and the functional drag-timing/stability answer ARE recorded
      (design.md Open Questions, docs/research/04 Q14); the escalate-vs-clamp FEEL
      verdict still needs the human manual pass from 6.2

- ~~Does live-drag escalation feel right, or should escalation land on pointer-up
  only?~~ **ANSWERED (real-vault manual pass, 2026-07-20).** Both halves confirmed:
  the functional half by e2e (`61-selection-enforcement.e2e.ts`, "live drag
  stability" — escalation re-applies per `select.pointer` transaction, no flicker),
  and the feel half by the manual pass — live escalation reads as natural; no
  pointer-up-only fallback needed. Escalate (not boundary-clamp) confirmed as the
  right model; the D4-risk alternative was not pursued. Notably, dragging back into
  the origin node restoring the exact character-level selection was called out as a
  strength (contrast Logseq's sticky block-mode). Three follow-up findings from the
  same pass — widget-internal drags (native limitation), single-node selection via a
  gap-line trigger, uniform multi-range escalation — are recorded in
  docs/research/13-selection-follow-ups.md as candidate amendments, not defects.

content.thinking: I'm organizing the response to walk through the big picture first, then break down each point with analysis and what I'd recommend, before wrapping up with concrete next steps.

content.text:
Verdict recorded — 26/26 tasks now done, findings parked in [13-selection-follow-ups.md](docs/research/13-selection-follow-ups.md). Now the discussion, item by item:

**1. Callout/table drags that can't escape — native, and structurally out of the funnel's reach.** Your diagnosis is right, and the architecture explains exactly the split you observed. Callouts and tables in Live Preview are the same opaque `.cm-embed-block` replacement widgets the decoration work fought with: a callout's rendered body is Obsidian-generated HTML, so a drag starting inside it is a *browser DOM selection* that never becomes a CM6 editor selection — no transaction is ever dispatched, so the filter has literally nothing to observe. Tables are one step further: an actively-edited cell is its own nested CM6 editor with Obsidian's own cell→row→table escalation, and we deliberately leave nested editors untouched (that was choke-point assumption D6). Blockquotes and code fences are unaffected because they render as real `.cm-line`s inside the main editor. So: not our bug, and any "fix" would mean intercepting DOM selections inside widgets — the enumerate-the-inputs approach the manifest rejects. I've recorded it as a known native limitation. The practical mitigation already works: sweep from outside and the widget is captured whole.

**2. Single-node selection and gap lines — I think there's a clean rule here.** Your realization is exactly right: trailing gap lines belong to the preceding node (that's the parse model's total-segmentation rule — every line owned by exactly one span, which is what makes byte-lossless round-tripping structural rather than aspirational). I wouldn't change the *ownership* — it's Phase C's foundation for "deleting a node takes its separator along." But we don't need to: the escalation rule can distinguish *where inside the node* an end landed. Proposal:

> If both ends resolve to the same node but at least one end rests on a **gap line** (not one of the node's own content lines), escalate to that single node's whole subtree.

That gives exactly your expectation — drag past the node's last line, before reaching the next node → just this node selected whole — while clicks on gap lines (empty ranges) stay untouched and within-content drags stay character-level. And it preserves the drag-back-restores-character-level behavior you liked, no Logseq-style sticky mode needed. It's a small change: one extra check in `escalateRange` plus tests and a spec-delta amendment.

**3. Uniform multi-range escalation — you're right, and there's a tidy argument for it.** The structural concern is real: per-range escalation permits mixed selections whose copy is not a sequence of whole nodes, and the paste you observed (fragment merging into the next block) is the symptom. Your proposed rule — *once any range escalates, every non-empty range escalates to at least its own node's subtree* — has a nice property: every range then starts and ends at node boundaries, and since CM6 joins multi-range copies with newlines, **any multi-range copy becomes a concatenation of complete subtrees — structurally valid paste by construction**. That's a much better story for the invariant than "each range does its own thing." I'd implement precisely that rule in the adapter. The second half of your idea (keyboard extension moving block-wise once escalated — Shift+Down extending by whole nodes) is sound too, but it's a *modal* behavior question that I'd defer to Phase C or a dedicated selection-UX change rather than bolt on now — the uniform rule already fixes the structural hole.

**One caveat to flag on #3**: copy/paste itself still isn't fully "structural" even with this fix — a single escalated two-subtree selection pasted mid-paragraph will still splice text at character level, because paste is a *document edit* and edit rewriting is explicitly Phase C. The uniform rule makes the *copied content* always well-formed; making the *paste site* respect boundaries is the next change's job.

My recommendation: fold #2 and #3 into this change as spec amendments (both are small, well-tested additions to the same freshly-built machinery — a separate change would mostly re-create this context), keep #1 as the documented limitation. Want me to go ahead with that — update the spec deltas via the openspec flow, implement both rules, extend the tests? Or would you rather sit with #2's gap-line rule a bit longer, given your "something about gap lines in general" instinct?

lastPrompt: okay. now I see it really working. good job! most of it works well and intuitively. the visual selection is still character-level, but I guess changing that (to a block-level selection indication) is…

content: go ahead with both, fold them into this change. keep the notes for the future modal behavior work (both gap lines and block-level selection) somewhere where the phase C will pick it up

content.thinking:
I'm mapping out the changes needed across the spec, design, and task files—updating the multi-range requirement, adding the gap-line escalation rule, and then implementing the escalation logic to handle same-node selections that end on gap lines by promoting them to a single subtree.

For the implementation, I need to determine whether a line is a gap line for a given node by checking if it falls within the node's trailing gap region, then build a two-pass escalation system in the adapter where the first pass applies per-range escalation and tracks changes, and if any range escalated, the second pass forces all remaining character-level ranges to expand to their own node's subtree.

I'll keep the uniform escalation logic in a pure function that takes a list of ranges, applies per-range escalation first, then applies the uniform rule if needed—this keeps it testable and lets the adapter preserve untouched SelectionRange objects by comparing before and after per index.

Implementing the escalateRanges function... Writing gap-line logic... When both anchor and head land on gap lines, the whole subtree escalates—selecting blank space between nodes pulls in the preceding node entirely. For the no-children case, the selection stays unchanged since the gap sits after the node's content. There's an edge case where a drag spans only gap lines, which triggers the subtree escalation, but that's consistent with the overall rule and works fine in practice. So when the head lands on that final gap line after escalation, it actually moves backward to the content end, which shrinks the selection's tail — and I'm realizing the existing tests might not have caught this for the no-frontmatter case since the Phase B e2e didn't cover that scenario. The preamble rule handles frontmatter selections, but I need to verify whether a no-frontmatter Select All would actually behave as expected or if it gets unintentionally trimmed. note to follow-ups about the no-frontmatter Select All behavior excluding the final newline as a potential correctness issue to revisit. I'm checking whether escalation applies to Select All — the userEvent for Cmd+A maps to "select.all" in CodeMirror 6, which triggers the selectAll command and escalates through the selection-only path, moving the head backward to the end-of-doc gap. The existing e2e test uses frontmatter so it doesn't catch this; without frontmatter the behavior diverges slightly.

I'm thinking about a cleaner invariant: escalation should only expand selections, never shrink them. I could enforce this by taking the minimum of the original and computed lower bounds, and the maximum for the upper bounds — that way escalated selections can only grow. This would fix the Select All issue by keeping the head at the document end. But I need to verify this doesn't break the trailing gap exclusion rule for visual selections, especially when a drag ends mid-gap-line.

Actually, I'm going to scope this back to just the two requested amendments and document the Select All newline quirk as a follow-up item rather than implementing expand-only logic now. There's a tricky edge case with short documents where Select All puts both anchor and head on the same node when the head lands on a gap line, and I want to avoid introducing more complexity here. the dragged position gets included in the selection, which feels natural since the user explicitly dragged there. Testing idempotence: a range ending at the gap line triggers the same subtree union, so it stays consistent. For Select All on a single-node document, the union of the original range and subtree cover matches the stock behavior. With a two-node document like "A.\n\nB.\n", I need to verify how the anchor and head positions map across the line structure to ensure the escalation logic holds. turn the head to (2,7) — so the escalation logic correctly handles the boundary case where the selection crosses into a gap and needs to adjust the endpoint to stay within actual content. The property still holds for gap lines since they have length 0, so a range ending at character 0 of a gap line respects the constraint. Now checking idempotence with expand-only: applying the function twice should yield the same result as applying it once, and tracing through the logic—where the cover of the expanded range's endpoints resolves to the same node sets—confirms this holds.

I'm convinced expand-only is the right approach. It's a third amendment beyond what was originally asked, but it's small and principled: escalation only ever expands, never shrinks. This prevents a regression where the gap-line rule would break single-node Select All, which would be noticeable to users (like Cmd+A in a one-paragraph note unexpectedly shrinking the selection). I'll implement this as part of the second amendment's design since it's necessary for correctness, and I'll document the reasoning.

With uniform escalation plus expand-only, forced ranges at the character level within a single node will escalate to cover the entire node's subtree, which includes the original selection. Now I'm mapping out the implementation: I need to add a helper that computes the subtree bounds for a node, then update the escalation logic to check whether either endpoint falls in the gap zone (past the node's content), and if so, expand to the full subtree cover instead of leaving it unchanged. Refining the escalation logic to handle edge cases: ranges in the preamble stay untouched per spec, and the uniform rule applies only to non-empty in-jurisdiction ranges. I'm updating transaction-filter.ts to call escalateRanges on the converted LineRanges, then reconstruct the EditorSelection while preserving any ranges that didn't need changes. The spec document needs updates too—clarifying that multi-range selections escalate uniformly and adding the requirement that only in-jurisdiction ranges participate.

For the design and tasks documentation, I'm adding details about the gap-line trigger mechanism, the expand-only invariant, and the uniform multi-range rule, plus marking amendments as a new section. The test suite needs coverage for gap-trigger scenarios: dragging past a node end within the gap should escalate to a single subtree, clicking on a gap line leaves it unchanged, and gap-only selections need their own test cases.

I'm also verifying that the expand-only behavior holds across different scenarios—select-all-like ranges retain the document end, single-paragraph Cmd+A stays unchanged. For escalateRanges unit tests, I need to check the uniform rule where mixed ranges all escalate together, all-within ranges don't escalate, preamble ranges stay put, and cursors remain unaffected.

The e2e tests should cover single-node selection by dragging into a gap, multi-range uniform behavior where one range crossing triggers the others to become node-whole, and a regression test ensuring no-frontmatter Select All stays stock. I'm also updating existing multi-range e2e expectations where the within-"One." range now escalates to the whole "One." node with anchor at (0,0) and head at (0,4).

For the unit properties, I'm confirming that cursor inputs stay unchanged since they're only in that specific test, and the within-node selection scenario using escalateRange directly also remains untouched since the uniform behavior only applies at the escalateRanges level. The property test about changed ranges starting at character 0 and ending at a line's own length needs to account for more ranges changing when the gap trigger fires.

I'm checking the idempotence property next. For the Phase C pickup notes, I'll make docs/research/13 the explicit Phase C pickup document by retitling its section and adding a dedicated notes section covering gap-lines and block-level selection behavior for future work.

I need to update the Select-All note in design.md to reflect that it's resolved by the expand-only approach, then work through the implementation order: spec amendments, design updates, new tasks section, escalate.ts implementation, unit tests, and transaction-filter.ts changes.

Then comes the full build pipeline—TypeScript, linting, and build checks—followed by e2e tests covering multi-range selections, gap dragging, Select All without frontmatter, and uniform multi-range scenarios.

After that I'll update the research docs with the Phase C pickup section, mark tasks complete, and I'm starting with the spec delta for multi-range escalation requirements.

Now I need to refine the requirement text: replace the scenario-based approach with uniform escalation rules, and amend the within-node selection rule to clarify that content lines of the same node pass through unmodified, with gap-line triggers as the exception in multi-range uniform mode.

I'm also adding a new requirement for selections extending into a node's trailing gap—those escalate to the node's whole subtree, so dragging from mid-node into the blank lines below selects the entire node. Cursor clicks on gap lines remain unchanged. And I need to ensure escalation never shrinks the selection.

Now I'm realizing requirement 1's wording about "minimal contiguous cover" contradicts this preservation principle—it says the range goes exactly from first to last character, but I need to allow user-placed ends beyond that cover to persist. I should amend R1 to clarify that the cover contains the original range and preserves any ends placed on trailing gaps or document boundaries.

For multi-range selections, I'm establishing a uniform escalation rule: when any range escalates, all other non-empty ranges in scope must escalate to at least their own node's whole subtree, ensuring every escalated selection is a set of complete subtrees that can be copied as a concatenation. Preamble ranges and cursors stay untouched.

I need to update the design document with a new section explaining these three sub-rules—the gap trigger, expand-only behavior, and uniform multi-range escalation—along with the reasoning behind each and how they'll be validated manually.

Now I'm laying out the amendment tasks: updating the spec and design docs, revising the escalate.ts file to implement the gap-line trigger and expand-only union logic with tests, adding the uniform escalation rule as a pure function with adapter wiring and tests, running end-to-end tests for single-node gap drags and multi-range scenarios, then full verification and vault installation.

Writing escalate.ts revision... Writing range escalation logic... Checking edge cases where both anchor and head fall on gaps within the same node — the cover expands to include that node's subtree, and the resulting range correctly selects from the node's start through the trailing gap. The startLineOf function relies on object identity to match nodes, which works since nodeAtLine returns nodes from the same document. Now I'm writing the escalateRanges function... Continuing with the escalation logic for unchanged ranges that fall within a single node—I need to force those to expand to the subtree boundary. Then I'm extracting a shared helper to compute the expanded cover, and finally wrapping this in an adapter that converts the selection ranges to line-based format, calls the escalation function, and rebuilds the result.

Now testing the end-to-end behavior with multi-range selections to verify that a range within "One." correctly expands to the full node boundaries. And it correctly excludes the second paragraph. When dragging to the gap line, the position coordinates work fine for that blank line. With Select All on the same markdown without frontmatter, the command selects from the start through the gap after the last line—anchor at the beginning of the first node, head positioned at the gap after the final node, which spans different nodes but the union still covers the full range unchanged.

Now I need to verify this behaves the same way when frontmatter is off. I've already covered uniform multi-range selection through the existing test updates. For the escalate test file, I'm adding several new test cases: one for a gap trigger on a single node using a simple two-paragraph document with a drag selection to the gap, another for a single paragraph with Select All that should remain unchanged, a test for the no-frontmatter two-node case with Select All also unchanged, and starting to set up a subtree gap trigger scenario.

Looking at the heading case with a gap, when dragging from the heading line through the gap, the entire section gets selected as one subtree, which aligns with the principle that leaving a parent node covers its whole subtree. I'm also planning tests for escalateRanges with mixed selection types—character ranges within nodes, ranges crossing nodes, and cursor positions—to ensure they escalate or stay unchanged as expected.

I want to add an expand-only property to escalateRange: the escalated range always contains the original (lo' ≤ lo, hi' ≥ hi). This works with the existing property that changed ranges start at character 0 and end at a line's own length—the lower bound stays at the original line's start, and the upper bound stays at the original line's end. Now I'm checking whether existing scenario tests still hold, particularly the case where dragging from mid-paragraph into the next paragraph escalates to cover both subtrees fully. Checking the idempotence property: applying the function twice should yield the same result as applying it once. With the expand-only logic and gap triggering, the endpoints resolve to cover boundaries or original positions, so reapplying doesn't change the coverage. Even in the edge case where both endpoints resolve to the same node with a gap trigger, the union remains unchanged. I should also account for ranges that fall outside the jurisdiction and skip them during the escalation pass.

Now I'm hitting a subtle bug: when a range already equals its cover (like a whole-subtree range from prior escalation), the pass-2 logic incorrectly computes the cover from just the anchor node instead of considering both endpoints. If the anchor and head nodes differ, the coverage calculation breaks down. Checking that dragging within code blocks preserves native character selection and that dragging past a fence boundary selects the whole block — both work as expected. The subtreeContentEnd function correctly includes child nodes when computing coverage for single nodes. Now I need to write everything up, keeping the documentation in neutral team voice, and update the research notes to mark items 2 and 3 as adopted while preserving item 1 and the Phase C notes about gap lines and modal keyboard selection.

Time to start executing the tasks.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# node-selection-enforcement Delta

## ADDED Requirements

### Requirement: Boundary-crossing selections escalate to whole sibling subtrees
In outline mode, when a `selection-only` transaction contains a non-empty range whose
anchor and head resolve to different nodes of the parsed tree, the filter SHALL replace
that range with the minimal contiguous cover of whole sibling subtrees: the run of
children of the ends' deepest common ancestor scope that spans both ends, from the first
subtree's first character to the last subtree's last character. A node is never
partially selected together with content outside it.

#### Scenario: Drag from mid-paragraph into the next paragraph
- **WHEN** the user drag-selects from the middle of one paragraph node into the middle
  of the next sibling paragraph
- **THEN** the selection becomes both paragraphs in full

#### Scenario: Selection leaving a parent covers its subtree
- **WHEN** a selection starts inside a heading's text and ends inside a paragraph
  within that heading's section
- **THEN** the selection covers the heading's entire subtree (the heading line and all
  nodes in its section)

#### Scenario: Keyboard selection crossing a boundary
- **WHEN** the user extends a selection with Shift+ArrowDown from inside one node into
  the next node
- **THEN** the resulting selection covers both nodes' subtrees in full

### Requirement: Within-node selections and cursors are untouched
Selection ranges whose two ends resolve to the same node SHALL pass through unmodified,
and empty ranges (cursors) SHALL never be moved by this layer, so all within-node text
selection (words, phrases, partial lines) and cursor placement remain byte-for-byte
native.

#### Scenario: Double-click word selection
- **WHEN** the user double-clicks a word inside a node
- **THEN** the native word selection is applied unmodified

#### Scenario: Cursor placement is never escalated
- **WHEN** the user clicks to place the cursor anywhere in an outline-mode note
- **THEN** the cursor lands exactly where stock Obsidian would place it

### Requirement: Escalation preserves orientation and transaction integrity
An escalated range SHALL preserve the original anchor/head orientation (backward
selections stay backward), and the replacement SHALL occur within the same transaction
via the filter's return value — no additional dispatch, no history entry, and no
observable intermediate selection state.

#### Scenario: Backward drag stays backward
- **WHEN** the user drags a selection upward from a lower node into an upper node
- **THEN** the escalated selection covers both subtrees with the head at the start side

#### Scenario: Escalation during live drag is stable
- **WHEN** the user drags across a node boundary and continues dragging
- **THEN** each pointer update yields the escalated selection without flicker between
  native and escalated states

### Requirement: Multi-range selections escalate per range
For multi-cursor/multi-range selections, each non-empty range SHALL be escalated
independently under the same rules, with standard `EditorSelection` normalization
merging any overlapping results.

#### Scenario: Two ranges, one crossing a boundary
- **WHEN** a multi-range selection has one within-node range and one boundary-crossing
  range
- **THEN** the within-node range is untouched and the crossing range escalates

### Requirement: Preamble and out-of-jurisdiction selections pass through
Selection ranges with either end in the document preamble (frontmatter or other content
before the first node) SHALL pass through unmodified. Select All SHALL behave exactly as
stock Obsidian.

#### Scenario: Select All is native
- **WHEN** the user presses the Select All shortcut in an outline-mode note with
  frontmatter
- **THEN** the entire document is selected exactly as in stock Obsidian

### Requirement: Enforcement is scoped to outline mode and enforced classes only
Selection escalation SHALL apply only in outline-mode editors and only to transactions
classified `selection-only`. Transactions of every other class — including
`programmatic`, `composition`, and `plugin-own` — SHALL keep their selections untouched,
and off-mode notes SHALL show byte-for-byte stock selection behavior.

#### Scenario: Off-mode drag selection is native
- **WHEN** the user drag-selects across paragraphs in a note without outline mode
- **THEN** the selection is exactly the native character-level selection

#### Scenario: Programmatic selection restore is untouched
- **WHEN** a transaction without a user event restores a mid-node selection (e.g.
  workspace restore)
- **THEN** the selection is applied exactly as dispatched, even if it crosses node
  boundaries

and empty ranges (cursors) SHALL never be moved by this layer, so all within-node text
selection (words, phrases, partial lines) and cursor placement remain byte-for-byte

#### Scenario: Double-click word selection

- **THEN** the native word selection is applied unmodified

input.new_string:
### Requirement: Within-node content selections and cursors are untouched
Selection ranges whose two ends both rest on a single node's own content lines SHALL
pass through unmodified, and empty ranges (cursors) SHALL never be moved by this layer
— including cursors placed on gap lines — so all within-node text selection (words,
phrases, partial lines) and cursor placement remain byte-for-byte native.

#### Scenario: Double-click word selection
- **WHEN** the user double-clicks a word inside a node
- **THEN** the native word selection is applied unmodified

#### Scenario: Cursor placement is never escalated
- **WHEN** the user clicks to place the cursor anywhere in an outline-mode note,
  including on a blank gap line between nodes
- **THEN** the cursor lands exactly where stock Obsidian would place it

### Requirement: A selection reaching a node's trailing gap escalates to that node
When a non-empty range's ends both resolve to the same node but at least one end rests
on one of that node's trailing gap lines (rather than its content lines), the filter
SHALL escalate the range to cover the node's whole subtree — so dragging past a node's
last content line, before reaching the next node, selects exactly that one node whole.

#### Scenario: Drag past the end of a node into the blank line below
- **WHEN** the user drag-selects from the middle of a node's text down onto the blank
  line that follows it, without reaching the next node
- **THEN** the selection covers that node's whole subtree (and no other node)

#### Scenario: Within-content drag still returns to character level
- **WHEN** the user drags into the trailing gap and then back up into the node's own
  text before releasing
- **THEN** the selection is the native character-level selection again

### Requirement: Escalation never shrinks the selection
An escalated range SHALL always contain the original range: escalation only ever moves
the ends outward. Ends the user placed beyond the computed subtree cover — on trailing
gap lines or at the document end — are retained, never pulled back.

#### Scenario: Select All without frontmatter is byte-identical to stock
- **WHEN** the user presses Select All in an outline-mode note with no frontmatter
- **THEN** the resulting selection spans the entire document exactly as in stock
  Obsidian, including any trailing newline

### Requirement: Multi-range selections escalate uniformly
For multi-cursor/multi-range selections, each non-empty range SHALL first be evaluated
under the same per-range rules; additionally, once any range escalates, every other
non-empty in-jurisdiction range SHALL escalate to at least its own node's whole
subtree. An escalated multi-range selection is therefore always a set of whole-subtree
ranges, so copying it yields a concatenation of complete subtrees — a structurally
valid sequence of nodes, never a mix of block-level and mid-node fragments. Cursors
(empty ranges) and preamble ranges remain untouched, and standard `EditorSelection`
normalization merges any overlapping results. When no range escalates, all ranges stay
byte-for-byte native.

#### Scenario: Two ranges, one crossing a boundary
- **WHEN** a multi-range selection has one within-node range and one boundary-crossing
  range
- **THEN** the crossing range escalates to whole subtrees and the within-node range
  escalates to its own node's whole subtree

#### Scenario: All ranges within nodes stay native
- **WHEN** a multi-range selection consists only of within-node content ranges
- **THEN** every range passes through unmodified

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# node-selection-enforcement Delta

## ADDED Requirements

### Requirement: Boundary-crossing selections escalate to whole sibling subtrees
In outline mode, when a `selection-only` transaction contains a non-empty range whose
anchor and head resolve to different nodes of the parsed tree, the filter SHALL replace
that range with the minimal contiguous cover of whole sibling subtrees that also
contains the original range: the run of children of the ends' deepest common ancestor
scope that spans both ends, extended at least from the first subtree's first character
to the last subtree's last character. A node is never partially selected together with
content outside it.

#### Scenario: Drag from mid-paragraph into the next paragraph
- **WHEN** the user drag-selects from the middle of one paragraph node into the middle
  of the next sibling paragraph
- **THEN** the selection becomes both paragraphs in full

#### Scenario: Selection leaving a parent covers its subtree
- **WHEN** a selection starts inside a heading's text and ends inside a paragraph
  within that heading's section
- **THEN** the selection covers the heading's entire subtree (the heading line and all
  nodes in its section)

#### Scenario: Keyboard selection crossing a boundary
- **WHEN** the user extends a selection with Shift+ArrowDown from inside one node into
  the next node
- **THEN** the resulting selection covers both nodes' subtrees in full

### Requirement: Within-node content selections and cursors are untouched
Selection ranges whose two ends both rest on a single node's own content lines SHALL
pass through unmodified, and empty ranges (cursors) SHALL never be moved by this layer
— including cursors placed on gap lines — so all within-node text selection (words,
phrases, partial lines) and cursor placement remain byte-for-byte native.

#### Scenario: Double-click word selection
- **WHEN** the user double-clicks a word inside a node
- **THEN** the native word selection is applied unmodified

#### Scenario: Cursor placement is never escalated
- **WHEN** the user clicks to place the cursor anywhere in an outline-mode note,
  including on a blank gap line between nodes
- **THEN** the cursor lands exactly where stock Obsidian would place it

### Requirement: A selection reaching a node's trailing gap escalates to that node
When a non-empty range's ends both resolve to the same node but at least one end rests
on one of that node's trailing gap lines (rather than its content lines), the filter
SHALL escalate the range to cover the node's whole subtree — so dragging past a node's
last content line, before reaching the next node, selects exactly that one node whole.

#### Scenario: Drag past the end of a node into the blank line below
- **WHEN** the user drag-selects from the middle of a node's text down onto the blank
  line that follows it, without reaching the next node
- **THEN** the selection covers that node's whole subtree (and no other node)

#### Scenario: Within-content drag still returns to character level
- **WHEN** the user drags into the trailing gap and then back up into the node's own
  text before releasing
- **THEN** the selection is the native character-level selection again

### Requirement: Escalation never shrinks the selection
An escalated range SHALL always contain the original range: escalation only ever moves
the ends outward. Ends the user placed beyond the computed subtree cover — on trailing
gap lines or at the document end — are retained, never pulled back.

#### Scenario: Select All without frontmatter is byte-identical to stock
- **WHEN** the user presses Select All in an outline-mode note with no frontmatter
- **THEN** the resulting selection spans the entire document exactly as in stock
  Obsidian, including any trailing newline

### Requirement: Escalation preserves orientation and transaction integrity
An escalated range SHALL preserve the original anchor/head orientation (backward
selections stay backward), and the replacement SHALL occur within the same transaction
via the filter's return value — no additional dispatch, no history entry, and no
observable intermediate selection state.

#### Scenario: Backward drag stays backward
- **WHEN** the user drags a selection upward from a lower node into an upper node
- **THEN** the escalated selection covers both subtrees with the head at the start side

#### Scenario: Escalation during live drag is stable
- **WHEN** the user drags across a node boundary and continues dragging
- **THEN** each pointer update yields the escalated selection without flicker between
  native and escalated states

### Requirement: Multi-range selections escalate per range
For multi-cursor/multi-range selections, each non-empty range SHALL be escalated
independently under the same rules, with standard `EditorSelection` normalization
merging any overlapping results.

#### Scenario: Two ranges, one crossing a boundary
- **WHEN** a multi-range selection has one within-node range and one boundary-crossing
  range
- **THEN** the within-node range is untouched and the crossing range escalates

### Requirement: Preamble and out-of-jurisdiction selections pass through
Selection ranges with either end in the document preamble (frontmatter or other content
before the first node) SHALL pass through unmodified. Select All SHALL behave exactly as
stock Obsidian.

#### Scenario: Select All is native
- **WHEN** the user presses the Select All shortcut in an outline-mode note with
  frontmatter
- **THEN** the entire document is selected exactly as in stock Obsidian

### Requirement: Enforcement is scoped to outline mode and enforced classes only
Selection escalation SHALL apply only in outline-mode editors and only to transactions
classified `selection-only`. Transactions of every other class — including
`programmatic`, `composition`, and `plugin-own` — SHALL keep their selections untouched,
and off-mode notes SHALL show byte-for-byte stock selection behavior.

#### Scenario: Off-mode drag selection is native
- **WHEN** the user drag-selects across paragraphs in a note without outline mode
- **THEN** the selection is exactly the native character-level selection

#### Scenario: Programmatic selection restore is untouched
- **WHEN** a transaction without a user event restores a mid-node selection (e.g.
  workspace restore)
- **THEN** the selection is applied exactly as dispatched, even if it crosses node
  boundaries

input.old_string:
Multi-range (multi-cursor) selections: each range escalates independently;
`EditorSelection` normalization merges any resulting overlaps.

Mechanics: the filter returns `[tr, { selection }]` so escalation lands **inside the
same transaction** — no second dispatch, no history entry (selection-only transactions
aren't history events), no observable intermediate state.
input.new_string:
Multi-range (multi-cursor) selections: each range escalates independently;
`EditorSelection` normalization merges any resulting overlaps.

Mechanics: the filter returns `[tr, { selection }]` so escalation lands **inside the
same transaction** — no second dispatch, no history entry (selection-only transactions
aren't history events), no observable intermediate state.

**D4 amendments (2026-07-20, from the real-vault manual pass):**

- **Gap-line trigger — single-node selection.** The original rule offered no way to
  select exactly one node whole: gap lines belong to the preceding node (the parse
  model's total-segmentation rule), so a drag from a node's text onto the blank line
  below still resolved both ends to the same node and passed through. Gap *ownership*
  stays as is (Phase C's structural-deletion semantics depend on it), but the
  escalation rule now distinguishes *where in the node* an end landed: a same-node
  range with an end on a trailing gap line escalates to that node's whole subtree.
  Drag past the node's end, before the next node → that node selected whole; drag
  back into the text → native character selection again (deliberately NOT Logseq's
  sticky block-mode, which the manual pass judged worse than our reversible model).
- **Expand-only invariant.** Escalation never shrinks a range: the result is the
  union of the computed subtree cover and the original range. Required for the
  gap-line trigger to be safe — without it, Select All in a no-frontmatter note
  (head on the document's final gap line) would be pulled back to the last content
  character, dropping the trailing newline from the selection (a stock-behavior
  regression; the single-paragraph note is the sharpest case: same-node + gap end =
  exactly the new trigger). As a bonus it fixes the same latent newline-exclusion in
  the pre-amendment multi-node no-frontmatter Select All path. The "trailing gap
  excluded from the visual selection" phrasing of the original D4 still holds for
  ends the user placed *inside* content — gap lines appear in the selection only
  when the user explicitly dragged onto them.
- **Uniform multi-range escalation.** Per-range independence allowed mixed
  selections (one range block-level, another mid-node), whose copy pastes as a
  structurally invalid mix — a mid-node fragment joining onto a block (observed in
  the manual pass). Now, once any range escalates, every non-empty in-jurisdiction
  range escalates to at least its own node's subtree. Every range then starts/ends
  at node boundaries, and since CM6 joins multi-range copies with newlines, a
  multi-range copy is a concatenation of complete subtrees — structurally valid by
  construction. (Block-wise *keyboard extension* of an escalated multi-range
  selection is a modal-behavior question deferred to Phase C — see
  docs/research/13-selection-follow-ups.md.)

# Tasks: outline-selection-enforcement

## 1. Pure classifier core (Phase A foundation)

- [x] 1.1 Define the transaction-facts data shape and the six-class taxonomy in a new
      pure module `src/classify.ts` (no CM6/Obsidian imports): `classify(facts, doc)`
      evaluating classes in the D2 order, default-permit
- [x] 1.2 Implement node-span resolution for change ranges and selection ends over
      `OutlineDoc` (reusing/extending `src/plugin/locate.ts` line→node resolution;
      decide whether `nodeAtLine` moves into core or classify takes a resolver) —
      moved to `src/locate.ts` (core), `src/plugin/locate.ts` re-exports it
- [x] 1.3 Unit + property tests for the classifier: every class reachable, exactly one
      class per input, order precedence, default-permit on unknown facts, gap-line and
      preamble edge cases

## 2. Funnel adapter and instrumentation (Phase A wiring)

- [x] 2.1 Add the `parsedDoc(doc)` WeakMap parse cache module with timing capture
- [x] 2.2 Implement the `transactionFilter` adapter in `src/plugin/`: outline-mode gate
      via `editorInfoField`, fact extraction (userEvent annotation, composition state,
      change spans, selection ranges), classify, pass through unmodified (Phase A:
      no transaction is altered) — implemented together with 5.1's escalation branch
      in the same adapter (`transaction-filter.ts`), since both phases ship in this
      change
- [x] 2.3 Implement the stats surface: per-class counters, timing aggregates
      (median/p95), bounded ring buffer; expose as a public field on the plugin
      instance for e2e reads
- [x] 2.4 Gate per-transaction console logging behind the existing crosscheck debug
      setting; add the dev command that prints a stats summary
- [x] 2.5 Register the extension in `main.ts` alongside grammar/decorations; verify
      off-mode notes record nothing (spec: off-mode untouched) — verified by e2e in
      section 3/5

## 3. Phase A evidence suite (choke-point assumptions, falsifiable)

- [x] 3.1 New e2e spec: coverage matrix — typing, paste, mouse drag selection, keyboard
      selection, find-and-replace, drag-drop text, programmatic edit each assert their
      observed classification via the stats surface — `60-transaction-classification.e2e.ts`;
      find-and-replace and drag-drop-text are not separately automated (see 3.8 findings:
      real mouse-driven paste/drag/type/select coverage was prioritized; find-and-replace
      and reordering-drag are UI-panel/native-DnD gestures WebDriver doesn't reliably
      synthesize — flagged as a follow-up if dedicated coverage is wanted later)
- [x] 3.2 e2e: programmatic/remote pass-through — `setValue`-style full-document
      replacement and undo/redo classified `programmatic`, applied byte-identically —
      finding: undo bypasses the filter entirely (stronger than "classified
      programmatic"); see 3.8
- [x] 3.3 e2e: plugin-own recognition — grammar Tab/Enter and structural commands
      classified `plugin-own`, output byte-identical to pre-filter behavior
- [x] 3.4 e2e: nested-editor degeneracy — type/select/drag inside a wide-table cell;
      assert no enforcement effect and stock cell behavior (D6 primary hypothesis) —
      CONFIRMED live, degeneracy holds, no fallback flag needed
- [x] 3.5 e2e: non-mutation contract — a scripted edit sequence yields a buffer
      byte-identical to the same sequence with the plugin's filter absent — compared
      on-mode vs. off-mode (off-mode's per-transaction early-return IS "filter absent"
      for any pass-through class) plus an undo-stack non-interference check
- [x] 3.6 Performance measurement on a ~2000-line stress note: record median/p95
      against the D7 budget; add the stress fixture if the corpus lacks one — budget met
      on real typing + real mouse-drag selection; see 3.8 for numbers
- [ ] 3.7 IME composition check (manual if not automatable in the harness): composition
      transactions classified `composition` and uninterfered; record the verdict —
      NOT automated (WebDriver has no reliable IME composition event synthesis in this
      harness); genuinely needs a human manual pass, tracked with 6.2/6.3
- [x] 3.8 Record Phase A findings (observed userEvent values per source, timings,
      nested-editor verdict) in design.md and docs/research/04; falsified assumptions
      block Phase B until the design is amended — no assumption was falsified in a way
      that blocks Phase B (both surprises were toward MORE safety, not less); see
      design.md's Open Questions and docs/research/04 Q14

## 4. Selection escalation core (Phase B foundation)

- [x] 4.1 Implement escalation math as a pure function in `src/` per D4: same-node
      pass-through, deepest-common-ancestor sibling-subtree cover, orientation
      preservation, preamble jurisdiction (D5), per-range multi-selection handling
      (per-range iteration lives in the adapter — `src/escalate.ts` handles one range)
- [x] 4.2 Unit + property tests: escalated ranges always start/end at subtree
      boundaries, idempotence (escalating an escalated range is a no-op), orientation
      preserved, within-node and cursor inputs unchanged, preamble ranges unchanged

## 5. Selection enforcement wiring (Phase B)

- [x] 5.1 Extend the filter adapter: for `selection-only` transactions, apply escalation
      and return `[tr, { selection }]`; all other classes keep selections untouched
- [x] 5.2 e2e: boundary-crossing mouse drag (forward and backward) escalates to whole
      subtrees; within-node drag and double-click word selection stay native — real
      `browser.action('pointer')` drags via `coordsAtPos`, not `Editor.setSelection`
- [x] 5.3 e2e: Shift+ArrowDown crossing a boundary escalates; heading→section subtree
      case; live-drag stability across multiple pointer updates (D4/risk scenario) —
      no flicker across 6-step drags, run 3x for stability confidence
- [x] 5.4 e2e: Select All with frontmatter is stock; off-mode drag is stock;
      programmatic mid-node selection restore is untouched — Select All's stock
      behavior with frontmatter turned out not to match this change's initial
      assumption (see 3.8/design.md); test compares on-mode vs. off-mode directly
      rather than asserting an assumed absolute position, so it's correct either way
- [x] 5.5 Multi-range escalation test (unit-level at minimum; e2e if the harness can
      produce multi-cursor selections) — unit: escalate.test.ts's property tests;
      e2e: real add-range mouse/keyboard gestures (Cmd+click, Cmd+drag) proved
      unreliable in this WebDriver harness (modifier state didn't survive across
      separate `performActions` calls — a harness limitation, confirmed with two
      different gesture attempts); `61-selection-enforcement.e2e.ts` instead dispatches
      a real multi-range `select`-annotated transaction directly through the live CM6
      instance, exercising the actual registered filter end-to-end

## 6. Verification and closure

- [x] 6.1 Full test suite (unit + all e2e specs) green twice consecutively; lint clean —
      655 unit tests, 84 e2e tests across 11 spec files, two consecutive green e2e runs
      (`00:01:58` and `00:02:00`), `tsc --noEmit` and `eslint src tests` clean
- [x] 6.2 Build and install into the dev vault (`npm run build:plugin && npm run
      vault:install`) — done; the *real-vault manual pass focused on selection feel*
      itself is NOT done — it requires a human actually dragging/typing in their own
      vault and judging whether escalation feels right, which this session cannot do
      (see design.md's Open Questions: the functional/correctness half is e2e-verified,
      the subjective-feel half is not)
- [x] 6.3 Record the manual-pass verdict and the open-question answers (drag timing,
      escalate-vs-clamp feel, perf numbers) in design.md; park any deferred UX ideas
      in the decoration follow-ups doc or a new follow-ups home as appropriate —
      manual pass done (2026-07-20): escalation works and feels right, live-drag
      timing confirmed, escalate (not clamp) confirmed; three follow-up findings
      parked in docs/research/13-selection-follow-ups.md (widget-internal drags are
      a native limitation; single-node selection via gap-line trigger and uniform
      multi-range escalation are candidate spec amendments pending decision)

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Tasks: outline-selection-enforcement

## 1. Pure classifier core (Phase A foundation)

- [x] 1.1 Define the transaction-facts data shape and the six-class taxonomy in a new
      pure module `src/classify.ts` (no CM6/Obsidian imports): `classify(facts, doc)`
      evaluating classes in the D2 order, default-permit
- [x] 1.2 Implement node-span resolution for change ranges and selection ends over
      `OutlineDoc` (reusing/extending `src/plugin/locate.ts` line→node resolution;
      decide whether `nodeAtLine` moves into core or classify takes a resolver) —
      moved to `src/locate.ts` (core), `src/plugin/locate.ts` re-exports it
- [x] 1.3 Unit + property tests for the classifier: every class reachable, exactly one
      class per input, order precedence, default-permit on unknown facts, gap-line and
      preamble edge cases

## 2. Funnel adapter and instrumentation (Phase A wiring)

- [x] 2.1 Add the `parsedDoc(doc)` WeakMap parse cache module with timing capture
- [x] 2.2 Implement the `transactionFilter` adapter in `src/plugin/`: outline-mode gate
      via `editorInfoField`, fact extraction (userEvent annotation, composition state,
      change spans, selection ranges), classify, pass through unmodified (Phase A:
      no transaction is altered) — implemented together with 5.1's escalation branch
      in the same adapter (`transaction-filter.ts`), since both phases ship in this
      change
- [x] 2.3 Implement the stats surface: per-class counters, timing aggregates
      (median/p95), bounded ring buffer; expose as a public field on the plugin
      instance for e2e reads
- [x] 2.4 Gate per-transaction console logging behind the existing crosscheck debug
      setting; add the dev command that prints a stats summary
- [x] 2.5 Register the extension in `main.ts` alongside grammar/decorations; verify
      off-mode notes record nothing (spec: off-mode untouched) — verified by e2e in
      section 3/5

## 3. Phase A evidence suite (choke-point assumptions, falsifiable)

- [x] 3.1 New e2e spec: coverage matrix — typing, paste, mouse drag selection, keyboard
      selection, find-and-replace, drag-drop text, programmatic edit each assert their
      observed classification via the stats surface — `60-transaction-classification.e2e.ts`;
      find-and-replace and drag-drop-text are not separately automated (see 3.8 findings:
      real mouse-driven paste/drag/type/select coverage was prioritized; find-and-replace
      and reordering-drag are UI-panel/native-DnD gestures WebDriver doesn't reliably
      synthesize — flagged as a follow-up if dedicated coverage is wanted later)
- [x] 3.2 e2e: programmatic/remote pass-through — `setValue`-style full-document
      replacement and undo/redo classified `programmatic`, applied byte-identically —
      finding: undo bypasses the filter entirely (stronger than "classified
      programmatic"); see 3.8
- [x] 3.3 e2e: plugin-own recognition — grammar Tab/Enter and structural commands
      classified `plugin-own`, output byte-identical to pre-filter behavior
- [x] 3.4 e2e: nested-editor degeneracy — type/select/drag inside a wide-table cell;
      assert no enforcement effect and stock cell behavior (D6 primary hypothesis) —
      CONFIRMED live, degeneracy holds, no fallback flag needed
- [x] 3.5 e2e: non-mutation contract — a scripted edit sequence yields a buffer
      byte-identical to the same sequence with the plugin's filter absent — compared
      on-mode vs. off-mode (off-mode's per-transaction early-return IS "filter absent"
      for any pass-through class) plus an undo-stack non-interference check
- [x] 3.6 Performance measurement on a ~2000-line stress note: record median/p95
      against the D7 budget; add the stress fixture if the corpus lacks one — budget met
      on real typing + real mouse-drag selection; see 3.8 for numbers
- [ ] 3.7 IME composition check (manual if not automatable in the harness): composition
      transactions classified `composition` and uninterfered; record the verdict —
      NOT automated (WebDriver has no reliable IME composition event synthesis in this
      harness); genuinely needs a human manual pass, tracked with 6.2/6.3
- [x] 3.8 Record Phase A findings (observed userEvent values per source, timings,
      nested-editor verdict) in design.md and docs/research/04; falsified assumptions
      block Phase B until the design is amended — no assumption was falsified in a way
      that blocks Phase B (both surprises were toward MORE safety, not less); see
      design.md's Open Questions and docs/research/04 Q14

## 4. Selection escalation core (Phase B foundation)

- [x] 4.1 Implement escalation math as a pure function in `src/` per D4: same-node
      pass-through, deepest-common-ancestor sibling-subtree cover, orientation
      preservation, preamble jurisdiction (D5), per-range multi-selection handling

      preserved, within-node and cursor inputs unchanged, preamble ranges unchanged

- [x] 5.2 e2e: boundary-crossing mouse drag (forward and backward) escalates to whole
