- * Escalate one selection range per D4. Returns `range` unchanged for:
- * empty ranges (cursors — never altered), ranges with either end in the
- * preamble (D5 jurisdiction), and within-node ranges (both ends resolve to
- * the same node). Otherwise expands to the minimal contiguous run of whole
- * sibling subtrees spanning both ends, orientation preserved.
+ * The expand-only invariant (D4 amendment): the escalated range is the
+ * UNION of the computed cover and the original range — escalation only
+ * ever moves ends outward, never pulls one back. Without this, an end the
+ * user placed beyond the cover (a trailing gap line, the document's final
+ * empty line) would be dragged back to the last content character —
+ * concretely, Select All in a no-frontmatter note would silently drop its
+ * trailing newline from the selection (sharpest in a single-node note,
+ * where the head-on-final-gap-line shape is exactly the gap-line trigger).
+ * Returns the ORIGINAL range object when the union changes nothing, so
+ * callers can use identity/equality to detect a real escalation.
+function expandToCover(range: LineRange, cover: Cover): LineRange {
+  const lo = isBackward(range) ? range.head : range.anchor;
+  const hi = isBackward(range) ? range.anchor : range.head;
+  const newLo = posBefore(cover.start, lo) ? cover.start : lo;
+  const newHi = posBefore(hi, cover.end) ? cover.end : hi;
+  if (posEqual(newLo, lo) && posEqual(newHi, hi)) return range;
+  return isBackward(range) ? { anchor: newHi, head: newLo } : { anchor: newLo, head: newHi };
+ * Escalate one selection range per D4 (as amended). Returns `range`
+ * unchanged for: empty ranges (cursors — never altered), ranges with
+ * either end in the preamble (D5 jurisdiction), and ranges whose ends both
+ * rest on a single node's own content lines. Escalates to the node's whole
+ * subtree when a same-node range has an end on a trailing gap line (the
+ * single-node-selection trigger), and to the minimal contiguous run of
+ * whole sibling subtrees when the ends resolve to different nodes — in
+ * both cases unioned with the original range (expand-only) and with
+ * orientation preserved.
export function escalateRange(doc: OutlineDoc, range: LineRange): LineRange {
if (isEmpty(range)) return range;
const anchorNode = nodeAtLine(doc, range.anchor.line);
const headNode = nodeAtLine(doc, range.head.line);
if (!anchorNode || !headNode) return range; // preamble jurisdiction (D5)

+ * Escalate a full selection's ranges with the uniform multi-range rule (D4
+ * amendment): every range is first escalated independently; if ANY range
+ * escalated, every other non-empty in-jurisdiction range is then escalated
+ * to at least its own node's whole subtree. The result is that an
+ * escalated multi-range selection is always a set of whole-subtree ranges
+ * — a multi-range copy concatenates complete subtrees, never a mix of
+ * block-level and mid-node fragments. Cursors and preamble ranges are
+ * never touched; when nothing escalates, all ranges come back unchanged.
+export function escalateRanges(doc: OutlineDoc, ranges: readonly LineRange[]): LineRange[] {
+  const escalated = ranges.map((range) => escalateRange(doc, range));
+  const anyEscalated = escalated.some((range, i) => !rangesEqual(range, ranges[i]!));
+  if (!anyEscalated) return escalated;
+  return escalated.map((range, i) => {
+    if (!rangesEqual(range, ranges[i]!)) return range; // already escalated
+    if (isEmpty(range)) return range; // cursors never move
+    const anchorNode = nodeAtLine(doc, range.anchor.line);
+    const headNode = nodeAtLine(doc, range.head.line);
+    if (!anchorNode || !headNode) return range; // preamble jurisdiction
+    // An unchanged non-empty in-jurisdiction range is a same-node content
+    // range — force it up to its node's whole subtree. (expandToCover also

content.thinking:
I'm spotting a potential bug in the `escalateRanges` uniform pass where if the anchor and head nodes differ but the range stays unchanged—which could happen with an already-perfect multi-node cover—the second pass would only grab the first subtree, then union it with the original spanning multiple subtrees, resulting in a no-op that's safe because we only expand, never shrink.

The comment about "same-node content range" isn't quite accurate for that edge case, but the behavior is correct and the expandToCover parenthetical already covers it, so I'll leave it as is.

Now I'm reviewing the unit tests in escalate.test.ts to check for any breakage: cursor preservation, within-node selections, mid-paragraph ranges, backward selections, parent-leaving cases, keyboard navigation, preamble tests, and multi-sibling scenarios all look good. For property tests, I need to verify idempotence still holds when ranges are built as (aLine,0)-(bLine,len(bLine)), and check that orientation is preserved when changed—though gap lines are blank with length 0, so empty ranges should skip. Even with gap lines that are single characters, the range still ends at the line's own length, which checks out. For the edge case where aLine and bLine are the same gap line, the union correctly produces a result that ends at that gap line's length. And the cursor input test with empty ranges at any line also validates as unchanged.

Now I need to write out the test cases covering gap-drag scenarios, drag-back-to-content, Select All across single and multi-node structures, heading gaps selecting whole sections, the containment property with escalation, and the escalateRanges function with mixed inputs, unchanged within-node cases, and cursor handling.
One subtle case in the uniform pass is handled by expand-only (an unchanged range that already exactly covers multiple subtrees stays a no-op since the union can't shrink it). Now the tests:

content.thinking:
I'm working through a counterexample with a markdown structure containing headings, list items, and code blocks to verify how the gap tracking works across different line types. The specific pick values and candidate resolution are helping me validate that all lines from 0 to 7 can be properly resolved through the heading's gap mechanism.

Now I'm computing the actual line indices using modulo arithmetic to determine which lines the anchor and head positions fall on, then checking whether they're contained within the code block's content range. I'm realizing my test's boundary computation was off—I calculated the range endpoints incorrectly by not accounting for the actual anchor and head positions properly.

content.text: The property test's own lo/hi math was wrong for backward ranges (the code is fine — an unchanged range failed the test's incorrectly-computed containment bounds). Fixing the test:

a.line < b.line || (a.line === b.line && a.ch <= b.ch);
+    const sorted = (r: LineRange): [LinePos, LinePos] =>
+      notAfter(r.anchor, r.head) ? [r.anchor, r.head] : [r.head, r.anchor];
fc.assert(
fc.property(arbTree(), fc.nat(), fc.nat(), (tree, aPick, bPick) => {
const text = encode(tree);
const bLine = candidates[bPick % candidates.length]!;
const r = range(pos(aLine, 0), pos(bLine, (lines[bLine] ?? '').length));
const result = escalateRange(doc, r);
-        const lo = pos(Math.min(aLine, bLine), 0);
-        const hi =
-          aLine >= bLine ? pos(aLine, (lines[aLine] ?? '').length) : pos(bLine, (lines[bLine] ?? '').length);
-        const resLo = result.head.line < result.anchor.line ||
-          (result.head.line === result.anchor.line && result.head.ch < result.anchor.ch)
-          ? result.head
-          : result.anchor;
-        const resHi = resLo === result.head ? result.anchor : result.head;
-        return before(resLo, lo) && before(hi, resHi);
+        const [lo, hi] = sorted(r);
+        const [resLo, resHi] = sorted(result);
+        return notAfter(resLo, lo) && notAfter(hi, resHi);

input.content:
# Selection-enforcement follow-ups and Phase C pickup notes

Findings from the first real-vault manual pass of `outline-selection-enforcement`
(2026-07-20), and the deferred design threads Phase C (edit rewriting/vetoing) should
pick up. The core verdict was positive: escalation works, live-drag is stable,
within-node selection and return-to-character-level behavior feel right.

## Resolved by amendment in the same change (2026-07-20)

Two of the original findings were adopted as D4 amendments rather than deferred —
see design.md's "D4 amendments" and the amended node-selection-enforcement delta spec:

- **Single-node selection via the gap-line trigger.** A same-node selection with an
  end on a trailing gap line escalates to that one node's whole subtree — dragging
  past a node's end, before the next node, selects exactly that node. Gap *ownership*
  (trailing gap belongs to the preceding node) is unchanged.
- **Uniform multi-range escalation.** Once any range escalates, every non-empty
  in-jurisdiction range escalates to at least its own node's subtree, so a multi-range
  copy is always a concatenation of complete subtrees — the mixed
  block-level/mid-node-fragment copy observed in the manual pass can no longer occur.
- **Expand-only invariant** (a required companion to the gap-line trigger): escalation
  never shrinks a range, which keeps no-frontmatter Select All byte-identical to stock
  (and fixed a latent trailing-newline exclusion in the pre-amendment behavior).

## Known native limitation (not ours to fix)

**Drags starting inside a rendered callout/table can't escape the widget.** In Live
Preview, when the cursor is outside a callout/table, the block is an opaque
`.cm-embed-block` replacement widget: a drag starting inside its rendered content is a
browser DOM selection that never becomes a main-editor CM6 selection, so no
transaction exists for the funnel to normalize. Table cells being edited are separate
nested CM6 editors with Obsidian's own cell→row→table selection escalation,
deliberately untouched (D6 degeneracy). Confirmed stock: reproduces with outline mode
off and with the plugin disabled. Blockquotes and code fences are unaffected (they
render as real `.cm-line`s). Any fix would mean DOM-level selection interception —
the enumerate-the-inputs architecture the manifest rejects — or an upstream Obsidian
change. Mitigation that already works: sweep from outside the widget and it is
selected whole.

## Phase C pickup notes

Threads deliberately deferred from the selection change, to be picked up when the
edit-rewriting change is specified:

- **Paste-site structural handling.** The uniform multi-range rule guarantees the
  *copied* content is a valid sequence of whole subtrees, but pasting is a document
  edit: pasted block content still splices at character level into the target
  position (observed: a block-level copy pasted mid-node merges with the surrounding
  paragraph). Phase C's paste re-indentation / boundary-respecting insertion is where
  this closes.
- **Modal block-level keyboard selection.** Once a selection is escalated, keyboard
  extension (Shift+Down etc.) currently moves the underlying character cursor and
  re-escalates per transaction — which works, but a true block-selection mode would
  extend by whole sibling subtrees per keypress, at every range of a multi-range
  selection simultaneously. This is a modal-behavior design (when to enter/leave the
  mode, how it interacts with the reversible drag-back behavior the manual pass
  praised) — spec it deliberately, not as a patch on the current rule.
- **Gap lines in general.** The manual pass surfaced a broader unease: gap lines
  *look* like inert empty space between blocks (no indentation, no visual ownership)
  but *are* owned by the preceding node in the model — the gap-line escalation
  trigger papers over one symptom, not the concept. Candidate directions when
  structural deletion (Phase C) makes gap semantics user-visible: visual gap
  ownership cues (a decoration-layer idea — see also docs/research/12), collapsing
  multi-blank gaps on structural moves, and whether cursor placement on gap lines
  should snap or stay free. Whatever Phase C decides about "deleting a node takes its
  trailing gap along" should be reconciled with how selections over gaps behave.
- **Escalated-selection visual treatment.** The manual pass noted selection still
  *renders* as character-level highlight even when escalated to whole nodes; a
  block-level selection indication (whole-node highlight chrome) was judged out of
  scope for the enforcement change — it belongs with the decoration/polish layer
  (docs/research/12) but becomes more valuable once Phase C makes escalated
  selections the operand of structural edits.

# Selection-enforcement follow-ups and Phase C pickup notes

Findings from the first real-vault manual pass of `outline-selection-enforcement`
(2026-07-20), and the deferred design threads Phase C (edit rewriting/vetoing) should
pick up. The core verdict was positive: escalation works, live-drag is stable,
within-node selection and return-to-character-level behavior feel right.

## Resolved by amendment in the same change (2026-07-20)

Two of the original findings were adopted as D4 amendments rather than deferred —
see design.md's "D4 amendments" and the amended node-selection-enforcement delta spec:

- **Single-node selection via the gap-line trigger.** A same-node selection with an
  end on a trailing gap line escalates to that one node's whole subtree — dragging
  past a node's end, before the next node, selects exactly that node. Gap *ownership*
  (trailing gap belongs to the preceding node) is unchanged.
- **Uniform multi-range escalation.** Once any range escalates, every non-empty
  in-jurisdiction range escalates to at least its own node's subtree, so a multi-range
  copy is always a concatenation of complete subtrees — the mixed
  block-level/mid-node-fragment copy observed in the manual pass can no longer occur.
- **Expand-only invariant** (a required companion to the gap-line trigger): escalation
  never shrinks a range, which keeps no-frontmatter Select All byte-identical to stock
  (and fixed a latent trailing-newline exclusion in the pre-amendment behavior).

## Known native limitation (not ours to fix)

**Drags starting inside a rendered callout/table can't escape the widget.** In Live
Preview, when the cursor is outside a callout/table, the block is an opaque
`.cm-embed-block` replacement widget: a drag starting inside its rendered content is a
browser DOM selection that never becomes a main-editor CM6 selection, so no
transaction exists for the funnel to normalize. Table cells being edited are separate
nested CM6 editors with Obsidian's own cell→row→table selection escalation,
deliberately untouched (D6 degeneracy). Confirmed stock: reproduces with outline mode
off and with the plugin disabled. Blockquotes and code fences are unaffected (they
render as real `.cm-line`s). Any fix would mean DOM-level selection interception —
the enumerate-the-inputs architecture the manifest rejects — or an upstream Obsidian
change. Mitigation that already works: sweep from outside the widget and it is
selected whole.

## Phase C pickup notes

Threads deliberately deferred from the selection change, to be picked up when the
edit-rewriting change is specified:

- **Paste-site structural handling.** The uniform multi-range rule guarantees the
  *copied* content is a valid sequence of whole subtrees, but pasting is a document
  edit: pasted block content still splices at character level into the target
  position (observed: a block-level copy pasted mid-node merges with the surrounding
  paragraph). Phase C's paste re-indentation / boundary-respecting insertion is where
  this closes.
- **Modal block-level keyboard selection.** Once a selection is escalated, keyboard
  extension (Shift+Down etc.) currently moves the underlying character cursor and
  re-escalates per transaction — which works, but a true block-selection mode would
  extend by whole sibling subtrees per keypress, at every range of a multi-range
  selection simultaneously. This is a modal-behavior design (when to enter/leave the
  mode, how it interacts with the reversible drag-back behavior the manual pass
  praised) — spec it deliberately, not as a patch on the current rule.
- **Gap lines in general.** The manual pass surfaced a broader unease: gap lines
  *look* like inert empty space between blocks (no indentation, no visual ownership)
  but *are* owned by the preceding node in the model — the gap-line escalation
  trigger papers over one symptom, not the concept. Candidate directions when
  structural deletion (Phase C) makes gap semantics user-visible: visual gap
  ownership cues (a decoration-layer idea — see also docs/research/12), collapsing
  multi-blank gaps on structural moves, and whether cursor placement on gap lines
  should snap or stay free. Whatever Phase C decides about "deleting a node takes its
  trailing gap along" should be reconciled with how selections over gaps behave.
- **Escalated-selection visual treatment.** The manual pass noted selection still
  *renders* as character-level highlight even when escalated to whole nodes; a
  block-level selection indication (whole-node highlight chrome) was judged out of
  scope for the enforcement change — it belongs with the decoration/polish layer
  (docs/research/12) but becomes more valuable once Phase C makes escalated
  selections the operand of structural edits.

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Selection-enforcement follow-ups (parking lot)

Findings from the first real-vault manual pass of `outline-selection-enforcement`
(2026-07-20). The core verdict was positive: escalation works, live-drag is stable,
within-node selection and return-to-character-level behavior feel right. These are the
items that surfaced as not-quite-right or worth a later round, parked here so they
don't silently evaporate.

## 1. Drags starting inside a rendered callout/table can't escape the widget

Observed: in Live Preview, when the cursor is *outside* a callout/table (so the block
is widget-rendered), starting a mouse drag *inside* the rendered block selects only
within the block, no matter how far the pointer leaves it — no escalation, no crossing
into neighboring nodes. Blockquotes and code fences are unaffected. Starting the drag
*outside* and sweeping over the widget selects it whole, as expected.

Root cause (architecture, not a bug in the funnel): these are the same opaque
`.cm-embed-block` replacement widgets the decoration work mapped out
(src/plugin/decorations.ts module comment). A callout's rendered content is
Obsidian-generated HTML — a drag inside it is a native browser DOM selection that
never becomes a main-editor CM6 selection, so no transaction is ever dispatched and
the transactionFilter has nothing to observe. A table cell being edited is its own
nested CM6 editor with its own selection (and Obsidian's own cell→row→table
escalation UI); its transactions are the nested editor's, deliberately left untouched
(D6 degeneracy). Confirmed stock: the same trapped-selection behavior reproduces with
outline mode off, and with the plugin disabled.

Disposition: out of jurisdiction for the transaction funnel by construction — there is
no transaction to normalize. Any fix would be a different mechanism (e.g. DOM-level
selection interception on widget content), which is the enumerate-the-inputs
architecture the manifest rejects, or an upstream Obsidian behavior. Revisit only if a
concrete node-selection UX (visual block selection layer) needs it; record as a known
limitation until then.

## 2. No way to select a single node whole; gap-line ownership makes it awkward

Observed: escalation only fires when a selection crosses into a *different* node, so
the minimum escalated selection is two subtrees. Dragging past a node's last line into
the blank line(s) below it does nothing — because trailing gap lines are owned by the
preceding node (the parse model's total-segmentation rule), so both selection ends
still resolve to the same node and the range passes through untouched.

Candidate rule (not yet decided): keep gap ownership as is, but treat "a selection end
rests on a gap line" as an escalation trigger even when both ends resolve to the same
node — escalating to that single node's whole subtree. That yields exactly "drag past
the end of the node, before reaching the next one → the node is selected whole," while
clicks (empty ranges) on gap lines stay untouched and within-content drags stay
character-level.

Comparison note (Logseq): Logseq switches to a sticky block-selection mode once a drag
leaves the origin block — dragging back keeps the origin block selected whole, which
gives single-block selection but loses the ability to return to character-level
without restarting the selection. Our current behavior (dragging back restores the
exact character-level selection) was judged nicer and safer in the manual pass; the
gap-line trigger would provide single-node selection without adopting the sticky mode.

Open question underneath: whether gap lines should participate in selection/visual
structure at all long-term ("do something about gap lines in general") — touches
decoration territory (docs/research/12) as much as enforcement.

## 3. Mixed multi-range selections can produce structurally odd copies

Observed: per-range escalation (the shipped spec behavior) allows a mixed selection —
one range escalated to whole subtrees, another still character-level inside a node.
Copying such a selection and pasting joins the character-level fragment onto the next
block of the pasted content, forming a new merged paragraph. Not wrong per se, but it
means an "outline-mode selection" can produce a paste that is not a sequence of whole
nodes, which sits uneasily next to the structural-boundaries principle.

Candidate rule (not yet decided): uniform escalation — once *any* range in the
selection escalates, every non-empty range escalates to at least its own node's whole
subtree. All ranges then start/end at node boundaries, so a multi-range copy is always
a concatenation of complete subtrees (CM6 joins multi-range copies with newlines) — a
structurally valid paste by construction. A further idea from the same discussion —
keyboard extension behaving block-wise once escalated (Shift+Down extends each range
by whole blocks) — is a modal-behavior question that belongs with Phase C / a
dedicated selection-UX round, not a tweak to the current rule.

## Verdict summary for the change record

- Live-drag escalation timing: feels right as shipped; no pointer-up-only fallback
  needed.
- Escalate vs. boundary-clamp: escalation confirmed as the right model; clamp
  alternative not pursued.
- Items 2 and 3 are candidate spec amendments (single-node selection via gap-line
  trigger; uniform multi-range escalation) pending a design decision; item 1 is a
  documented native limitation.

lastPrompt: go ahead with both, fold them into this change. keep the notes for the future modal behavior work (both gap lines and block-level selection) somewhere where the phase C will pick it up

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Tasks: outline-selection-enforcement

## 1. Pure classifier core (Phase A foundation)

- [x] 1.1 Define the transaction-facts data shape and the six-class taxonomy in a new
      pure module `src/classify.ts` (no CM6/Obsidian imports): `classify(facts, doc)`
      evaluating classes in the D2 order, default-permit
- [x] 1.2 Implement node-span resolution for change ranges and selection ends over
      `OutlineDoc` (reusing/extending `src/plugin/locate.ts` line→node resolution;
      decide whether `nodeAtLine` moves into core or classify takes a resolver) —
      moved to `src/locate.ts` (core), `src/plugin/locate.ts` re-exports it
- [x] 1.3 Unit + property tests for the classifier: every class reachable, exactly one
      class per input, order precedence, default-permit on unknown facts, gap-line and
      preamble edge cases

## 2. Funnel adapter and instrumentation (Phase A wiring)

- [x] 2.1 Add the `parsedDoc(doc)` WeakMap parse cache module with timing capture
- [x] 2.2 Implement the `transactionFilter` adapter in `src/plugin/`: outline-mode gate
      via `editorInfoField`, fact extraction (userEvent annotation, composition state,
      change spans, selection ranges), classify, pass through unmodified (Phase A:
      no transaction is altered) — implemented together with 5.1's escalation branch
      in the same adapter (`transaction-filter.ts`), since both phases ship in this
      change
- [x] 2.3 Implement the stats surface: per-class counters, timing aggregates
      (median/p95), bounded ring buffer; expose as a public field on the plugin
      instance for e2e reads
- [x] 2.4 Gate per-transaction console logging behind the existing crosscheck debug
      setting; add the dev command that prints a stats summary
- [x] 2.5 Register the extension in `main.ts` alongside grammar/decorations; verify
      off-mode notes record nothing (spec: off-mode untouched) — verified by e2e in
      section 3/5

## 3. Phase A evidence suite (choke-point assumptions, falsifiable)

- [x] 3.1 New e2e spec: coverage matrix — typing, paste, mouse drag selection, keyboard
      selection, find-and-replace, drag-drop text, programmatic edit each assert their
      observed classification via the stats surface — `60-transaction-classification.e2e.ts`;
      find-and-replace and drag-drop-text are not separately automated (see 3.8 findings:
      real mouse-driven paste/drag/type/select coverage was prioritized; find-and-replace
      and reordering-drag are UI-panel/native-DnD gestures WebDriver doesn't reliably
      synthesize — flagged as a follow-up if dedicated coverage is wanted later)
- [x] 3.2 e2e: programmatic/remote pass-through — `setValue`-style full-document
      replacement and undo/redo classified `programmatic`, applied byte-identically —
      finding: undo bypasses the filter entirely (stronger than "classified
      programmatic"); see 3.8
- [x] 3.3 e2e: plugin-own recognition — grammar Tab/Enter and structural commands
      classified `plugin-own`, output byte-identical to pre-filter behavior
- [x] 3.4 e2e: nested-editor degeneracy — type/select/drag inside a wide-table cell;
      assert no enforcement effect and stock cell behavior (D6 primary hypothesis) —

content.content: Updated task #19 status

content.text:
Both amendments are in, verified, and installed in your dev vault — thanks to hot-reload, the new build should already be live (or will be the moment Obsidian sees the changed `main.js`).

## What changed

**Single-node selection (gap-line trigger).** Dragging from a node's text past its last line onto the blank line below — before reaching the next node — now selects exactly that node whole. Dragging back into the text returns to character-level, keeping the reversible behavior you preferred over Logseq's sticky mode. Gap *ownership* is untouched (Phase C's structural-deletion semantics still depend on it); only the escalation rule now looks at *where inside the node* an end landed.

**Uniform multi-range escalation.** Once any range escalates, every non-empty range escalates to at least its own node's whole subtree. So a multi-range copy is now always a concatenation of complete subtrees — the mixed fragment-joins-next-block paste you observed can't occur anymore. (Block-wise keyboard *extension* of an escalated selection is deferred to Phase C as a modal-behavior design.)

**One companion invariant I had to add: escalation never shrinks a selection.** Working through the gap-line trigger I found it would have regressed Select All in notes without frontmatter — Cmd+A puts the head on the document's final blank line, which is exactly the "same node, end on gap" shape, and naive escalation would pull the head back to the last content character, dropping the trailing newline. The fix: the escalated range is always the *union* of the subtree cover and the original range. This also fixed the same latent newline-exclusion that already existed in the multi-node Select All path. It's specced, unit-tested (including a new containment property over generated trees), and e2e-tested against off-mode as the reference.

**Verification**: 667 unit tests, 89 e2e tests across 11 specs (4 new/updated for the amendments, including a real mouse gap-drag), lint/typecheck clean, `openspec validate` passes, spec deltas + design.md D4 amended.

**Phase C pickup**: [13-selection-follow-ups.md](docs/research/13-selection-follow-ups.md) is now structured as the Phase C input doc — paste-site structural handling, modal block-level keyboard selection, gap lines in general, and the block-level selection *visual* treatment you mentioned at the start, plus the callout/table widget limitation as a documented native constraint.

content:
ok. great. both improvements seem to work well and feel intuitive.

some comments for further discussion before we wrap this up:

* Single-node selection: we don't have gap lines between list elements, so it doesn't seem to be possible to select a single list node as a whole. any ideas on how to improve this?
* related to the above, I'd like to propose a change to the select-all spec:
   * one of the improvements that obsidian-outliner does is changing the cmd-A behavior: "Press the hotkey once to select the current list item. Press the hotkey twice to select the entire list".
   * I would generalize that to the same behavior as in Logseq (btw, is this consistent with Workflowy?)
      * the hotkey first selects the whole text _inside_ the node
      * next it escalates to block-selection of the node as a whole (together with its subtree)
      * next it escalates to the node's parent subtree
      * and so on, until the whole outline, then the whole document (together with the frontmatter)
   * this should gracefully generalize for multi-select, joining selection when the escalated subtrees overlap

message.content:
ok. great. both improvements seem to work well and feel intuitive.

some comments for further discussion before we wrap this up:

* Single-node selection: we don't have gap lines between list elements, so it doesn't seem to be possible to select a single list node as a whole. any ideas on how to improve this?
* related to the above, I'd like to propose a change to the select-all spec:
   * one of the improvements that obsidian-outliner does is changing the cmd-A behavior: "Press the hotkey once to select the current list item. Press the hotkey twice to select the entire list".
   * I would generalize that to the same behavior as in Logseq (btw, is this consistent with Workflowy?)
      * the hotkey first selects the whole text _inside_ the node
      * next it escalates to block-selection of the node as a whole (together with its subtree)
      * next it escalates to the node's parent subtree
      * and so on, until the whole outline, then the whole document (together with the frontmatter)
   * this should gracefully generalize for multi-select, joining selection when the escalated subtrees overlap

content.thinking:
The user is raising two design challenges: first, how to select a single list item when there are no gap lines between items to trigger the selection, and second, implementing a progressive Select All that escalates through nested levels—from text within a node up through subtrees, parent subtrees, and eventually the whole document. They're thinking about how this could generalize existing outliner patterns from Obsidian and Logseq while handling multi-select merging gracefully.

Progressive Select All would be a keymap-level feature—intercepting Cmd+A at high precedence to compute the next escalation level based on current selection state, which is more substantial than the previous amendments but still well-scoped. Interestingly, the progressive Cmd+A actually solves the single-item selection problem: pressing once inside an item selects its text, pressing again selects the node plus subtree. For mouse-only workflows on list items, there are other options being considered, like dragging past the end of a line to trigger escalation, or using triple-click to select a full line and potentially escalate from there—though distinguishing intentional full-line selections from accidental ones is tricky.

The cleaner approach is clicking the bullet marker itself, which feels natural for selecting a node's subtree, though that's a decoration-level interaction to handle later. For keyboard, repeated Cmd+A already provides progressive selection, and Phase C's modal block selection would cover this too. Workflowy's behavior with Cmd+A—selecting the item's text first, then expanding to the level or whole list on subsequent presses—offers a useful precedent. I'm thinking through the selection ladder logic for progressive Cmd+A — it's actually stateless and computed from the current selection state. The first press selects the node's text content, the next escalates to the whole subtree, then to the parent's subtree, and eventually to the entire document. This is a separate interaction feature from the normalization work, so it might be better as its own change rather than folding it into the current large one. They're framing this as a discussion point before wrapping up, not a directive to implement immediately — they want to explore whether to fold the progressive select-all into this change or defer it as a follow-up. I should analyze both approaches and recommend one, then ask which direction they prefer.

Let me also check how Workflowy handles Cmd+A to ground my analysis, and think through the tight-list selection problem: progressive Cmd+A would give a keyboard path (cursor in item, press once for text, twice for subtree), which solves the single-node selection issue that mouse gestures can't cleanly handle. Regardless of gaps, progressive Cmd+A gives uniform single-node selection. For the implementation, I'm sketching a pure function that computes the next selection step by comparing the current range against node boundaries — the tricky part is deciding whether to include the list marker when selecting a node's content or just the text after it. Handling the whole document case and staying put, then intercepting Cmd+A in outline mode to merge overlapping ranges. This is a solid chunk of work—new spec requirement, pure function with tests, keymap binding, and end-to-end validation. It's comparable to the previous amendment round and arguably belongs in the keyboard grammar territory since it modifies the keymap, but I think it makes sense to fold it in as part of this amendment round since it completes the node selection capability.

content.text: Both points are good — and they're connected: the progressive Cmd+A actually *answers* the list-item problem. Let me quickly check the Workflowy question, then lay out my take.

Based on the search results, I found information about WorkFlowy's Ctrl+A behavior:

In WorkFlowy, pressing Ctrl+A selects a single line as usual. Pressing Ctrl+A again selects/highlights the entire page. This demonstrates the progressive select behavior you asked about.

Additionally, the keyboard shortcut sequence involves holding the Ctrl (Cmd) key and typing A-A-X, where the first A selects a line, the second A selects everything, and the X cuts it all to the clipboard.

This progressive selection system allows users to first select an individual item with one Ctrl+A press, and then select the entire list/page with a second Ctrl+A press, providing a convenient way to expand the selection scope without reaching for the mouse.

content.thinking:
Workflowy's selection works in two steps—current line then whole page—while Logseq progressively climbs through blocks and parents. The user's proposal generalizes both approaches into a consistent ladder, which aligns with Workflowy's spirit but offers finer control. Now I need to write up the discussion and recommendation, then ask whether this should fold into an existing change or stand separately.

This feature is substantial enough to warrant its own spec section—it's a new keymap surface that intercepts the select-all command and implements a multi-step interaction ladder rather than just transaction normalization. The mechanism differs from what the filter can handle since repeated presses need to cycle through states, requiring a keymap handler that runs before the native select-all triggers. I'm leaning toward folding it in since the change has already been through two verification rounds, though I should present both options to the user.

For the mouse-only selection problem, the progressive select-all handles the keyboard case, but mouse alternatives are trickier—triple-click escalation is risky as a heuristic, and tight list geometry means there's genuinely no in-between position for drag selection, which is why the interaction ladder and bullet-click gesture are the real solutions. The multi-select generalization works too: applying the ladder per range and letting CM6 normalization merge overlaps gives us uniform escalation across selections.

One key detail: the final ladder step is just native select-all (whole document with frontmatter), which passes through by design. The ladder itself is stateless—derived by comparing the current selection to what it covers—so there's no timing complexity, matching obsidian-outliner's robustness.

I should keep the response concise, cite sources for the Workflowy reference, then ask what scope the user wants to lock in.

content.text:
Glad both landed well. Here's my take on the two points — they turn out to be the same problem wearing two hats.

**1. Single list items: the geometry genuinely has no room for a mouse gesture.** In a tight list, the next sibling starts on the very next line — there is no in-between position to drag onto, so no drag-shape can mean "just this item" the way the gap-line trigger does for spaced blocks. (Loose lists — items with blank lines between them — already work today, since those gaps are owned by the items.) The two real answers are:

- **Your progressive Cmd+A — this is the keyboard answer, and it fully solves it**: cursor in item → press once (item text) → press again (item + subtree). No gap lines needed.
- For a *mouse* path, the natural gesture is **click-the-bullet/marker to select the subtree** (what Logseq/Workflowy do with their bullets). That's a DOM/decoration-layer interaction, not a transaction — it belongs with the decorations work, so I'd park it in the follow-ups doc rather than build it here.

**2. Progressive Select All: yes, and your generalization is the right shape.** On Workflowy: it's consistent in spirit but coarser — Workflowy's Ctrl+A is a two-step ladder (once = current line, twice = the whole page), per their own [keyboard shortcut posts](https://blog.workflowy.com/keyboard-shortcut-challenge/) and [hotkey hacks](https://blog.workflowy.com/workflowy-hotkey-hacks/). Your parent-by-parent ladder (Logseq-style) strictly generalizes both Workflowy's two-step and obsidian-outliner's item→list version, and degrades to them in shallow documents. A few design observations from thinking it through:

- **The ladder can be stateless** — no double-press timing needed. Each press compares the current selection against the ladder's rungs (node content span → node subtree cover → parent subtree cover → … → whole outline → whole document) and picks the next one up. That's robust (works after any interruption, no timers) and it's how obsidian-outliner does it too.
- **It's a keymap feature, not a filter feature** — this matters architecturally. Repeated Cmd+A presses produce *identical* native select-all transactions; the filter can't distinguish press one from press three. The ladder has to intercept Mod-A before dispatch, exactly like the grammar's Tab/Enter handlers (high-precedence keymap, outline-mode gated, pure planner function). So it lands in the keyboard-grammar mechanism, with the escalation covers reused from `escalate.ts`.
- **The last rung (whole document incl. frontmatter) is exactly native Select All**, which the filter already passes through — the ladder tops out into stock behavior, which is a nice consistency story.
- **Multi-range**: agreed it generalizes cleanly — each range steps its own ladder, CM6's selection normalization merges overlapping results, and the uniform-escalation rule already guarantees the merged result is whole subtrees.
- One spec detail to pin down: does "whole text inside the node" for a list item include the `- ` marker or start after it? I'd say after (content only), matching obsidian-outliner and reading better for copy — but worth deciding explicitly.

**On scope**: this is meatier than the last two amendments — a new keymap surface with its own spec requirement, planner function, and e2e round — and it lives in a different mechanism than the transaction filter this change is about. So it's a genuine judgment call whether to fold it in or make it a small follow-on change:

  selection simultaneously. This is a modal-behavior design (when to enter/leave the

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# Selection-enforcement follow-ups and Phase C pickup notes

Findings from the first real-vault manual pass of `outline-selection-enforcement`
(2026-07-20), and the deferred design threads Phase C (edit rewriting/vetoing) should
pick up. The core verdict was positive: escalation works, live-drag is stable,
within-node selection and return-to-character-level behavior feel right.

## Resolved by amendment in the same change (2026-07-20)

Two of the original findings were adopted as D4 amendments rather than deferred —
see design.md's "D4 amendments" and the amended node-selection-enforcement delta spec:

- **Single-node selection via the gap-line trigger.** A same-node selection with an
  end on a trailing gap line escalates to that one node's whole subtree — dragging
  past a node's end, before the next node, selects exactly that node. Gap *ownership*
  (trailing gap belongs to the preceding node) is unchanged.
- **Uniform multi-range escalation.** Once any range escalates, every non-empty
  in-jurisdiction range escalates to at least its own node's subtree, so a multi-range
  copy is always a concatenation of complete subtrees — the mixed
  block-level/mid-node-fragment copy observed in the manual pass can no longer occur.
- **Expand-only invariant** (a required companion to the gap-line trigger): escalation
  never shrinks a range, which keeps no-frontmatter Select All byte-identical to stock
  (and fixed a latent trailing-newline exclusion in the pre-amendment behavior).

## Known native limitation (not ours to fix)

**Drags starting inside a rendered callout/table can't escape the widget.** In Live
Preview, when the cursor is outside a callout/table, the block is an opaque
`.cm-embed-block` replacement widget: a drag starting inside its rendered content is a
browser DOM selection that never becomes a main-editor CM6 selection, so no
transaction exists for the funnel to normalize. Table cells being edited are separate
nested CM6 editors with Obsidian's own cell→row→table selection escalation,
deliberately untouched (D6 degeneracy). Confirmed stock: reproduces with outline mode
off and with the plugin disabled. Blockquotes and code fences are unaffected (they
render as real `.cm-line`s). Any fix would mean DOM-level selection interception —
the enumerate-the-inputs architecture the manifest rejects — or an upstream Obsidian
change. Mitigation that already works: sweep from outside the widget and it is
selected whole.

## Phase C pickup notes

Threads deliberately deferred from the selection change, to be picked up when the
edit-rewriting change is specified:

- **Paste-site structural handling.** The uniform multi-range rule guarantees the
  *copied* content is a valid sequence of whole subtrees, but pasting is a document
  edit: pasted block content still splices at character level into the target
  position (observed: a block-level copy pasted mid-node merges with the surrounding
  paragraph). Phase C's paste re-indentation / boundary-respecting insertion is where
  this closes.
- **Modal block-level keyboard selection.** Once a selection is escalated, keyboard
  extension (Shift+Down etc.) currently moves the underlying character cursor and
  re-escalates per transaction — which works, but a true block-selection mode would
  extend by whole sibling subtrees per keypress, at every range of a multi-range
  selection simultaneously. This is a modal-behavior design (when to enter/leave the
  mode, how it interacts with the reversible drag-back behavior the manual pass
  praised) — spec it deliberately, not as a patch on the current rule.
- **Gap lines in general.** The manual pass surfaced a broader unease: gap lines
  *look* like inert empty space between blocks (no indentation, no visual ownership)
  but *are* owned by the preceding node in the model — the gap-line escalation
  trigger papers over one symptom, not the concept. Candidate directions when
  structural deletion (Phase C) makes gap semantics user-visible: visual gap
  ownership cues (a decoration-layer idea — see also docs/research/12), collapsing
  multi-blank gaps on structural moves, and whether cursor placement on gap lines
  should snap or stay free. Whatever Phase C decides about "deleting a node takes its
  trailing gap along" should be reconciled with how selections over gaps behave.
- **Escalated-selection visual treatment.** The manual pass noted selection still
  *renders* as character-level highlight even when escalated to whole nodes; a
  block-level selection indication (whole-node highlight chrome) was judged out of
  scope for the enforcement change — it belongs with the decoration/polish layer
  (docs/research/12) but becomes more valuable once Phase C makes escalated
  selections the operand of structural edits.

praised) — spec it deliberately, not as a patch on the current rule.

message.content: I'm thinking, haven't we scope-creeped phase C? it was about edits rewriting/filtering. and now it includes substantial work on selections: block-level plus the select-all ladder. shouldn't those be separate?

content.thinking:
The user caught an issue where selection-UX work got mixed into Phase C's scope, but Phase C should only cover edit rewriting and vetoing. I need to separate these concerns and restructure the research docs accordingly.

Looking at what belongs where: paste-site structural handling stays in Phase C since it's an edit operation, gap-line deletion semantics is Phase C-core but the visual treatment is decoration, and everything else—modal block selection, select-all ladder, bullet-click, visual block indication—moves to its own "node-selection UX" track as a separate future change. I'll reorganize the doc to clearly split "Phase C (edit enforcement) inputs" from "Node-selection UX track" and add a header note explaining the distinction.

content.text: You're right — that's exactly what happened. Phase C was defined narrowly in the design (rewriting/vetoing *document edits*: boundary-crossing deletions, paste re-indentation, orphan prevention), and I parked a pile of selection-UX work under its name. Those are different tracks: only paste-site handling and the gap-deletion semantics genuinely feed edit enforcement. Let me restructure the doc:

content.content:
1	# Selection-enforcement follow-ups and Phase C pickup notes
2	
3	Findings from the first real-vault manual pass of `outline-selection-enforcement`
4	(2026-07-20), and the deferred design threads Phase C (edit rewriting/vetoing) should
5	pick up. The core verdict was positive: escalation works, live-drag is stable,
6	within-node selection and return-to-character-level behavior feel right.
7	
8	## Resolved by amendment in the same change (2026-07-20)
9	
10	Two of the original findings were adopted as D4 amendments rather than deferred —
11	see design.md's "D4 amendments" and the amended node-selection-enforcement delta spec:
12	
13	- **Single-node selection via the gap-line trigger.** A same-node selection with an
14	  end on a trailing gap line escalates to that one node's whole subtree — dragging
15	  past a node's end, before the next node, selects exactly that node. Gap *ownership*
16	  (trailing gap belongs to the preceding node) is unchanged.
17	- **Uniform multi-range escalation.** Once any range escalates, every non-empty
18	  in-jurisdiction range escalates to at least its own node's subtree, so a multi-range
19	  copy is always a concatenation of complete subtrees — the mixed
20	  block-level/mid-node-fragment copy observed in the manual pass can no longer occur.
21	- **Expand-only invariant** (a required companion to the gap-line trigger): escalation
22	  never shrinks a range, which keeps no-frontmatter Select All byte-identical to stock
23	  (and fixed a latent trailing-newline exclusion in the pre-amendment behavior).
24	
25	## Known native limitation (not ours to fix)
26	
27	**Drags starting inside a rendered callout/table can't escape the widget.** In Live
28	Preview, when the cursor is outside a callout/table, the block is an opaque
29	`.cm-embed-block` replacement widget: a drag starting inside its rendered content is a
30	browser DOM selection that never becomes a main-editor CM6 selection, so no
31	transaction exists for the funnel to normalize. Table cells being edited are separate
32	nested CM6 editors with Obsidian's own cell→row→table selection escalation,
33	deliberately untouched (D6 degeneracy). Confirmed stock: reproduces with outline mode
34	off and with the plugin disabled. Blockquotes and code fences are unaffected (they
35	render as real `.cm-line`s). Any fix would mean DOM-level selection interception —
36	the enumerate-the-inputs architecture the manifest rejects — or an upstream Obsidian
37	change. Mitigation that already works: sweep from outside the widget and it is
38	selected whole.
39	
40	## Phase C pickup notes
41	
42	Threads deliberately deferred from the selection change, to be picked up when the
43	edit-rewriting change is specified:
44	
45	- **Paste-site structural handling.** The uniform multi-range rule guarantees the
46	  *copied* content is a valid sequence of whole subtrees, but pasting is a document
47	  edit: pasted block content still splices at character level into the target
48	  position (observed: a block-level copy pasted mid-node merges with the surrounding
49	  paragraph). Phase C's paste re-indentation / boundary-respecting insertion is where
50	  this closes.
51	- **Modal block-level keyboard selection.** Once a selection is escalated, keyboard
52	  extension (Shift+Down etc.) currently moves the underlying character cursor and
53	  re-escalates per transaction — which works, but a true block-selection mode would
54	  extend by whole sibling subtrees per keypress, at every range of a multi-range
55	  selection simultaneously. This is a modal-behavior design (when to enter/leave the
56	  mode, how it interacts with the reversible drag-back behavior the manual pass
57	  praised) — spec it deliberately, not as a patch on the current rule.
58	- **Progressive Select All (the selection ladder).** Design discussed and agreed
59	  after the second manual pass (2026-07-20), deliberately parked here rather than
60	  amended in. Repeated Cmd+A presses climb a ladder: the node's own content text →
61	  the node's whole subtree → the parent's subtree → … → the whole outline → the
62	  whole document including frontmatter (which is exactly native Select All, so the
63	  ladder tops out into stock behavior the filter already passes through). Design
64	  decisions already made:
65	  - *Stateless*: each press compares the current selection against the ladder's
66	    rungs and picks the next one up — no double-press timers; robust after any
67	    interruption. Same approach obsidian-outliner uses for its two-step version
68	    ("once = current list item, twice = entire list").
69	  - *Mechanism*: a high-precedence keymap handler like the grammar's Tab/Enter — NOT
70	    the transaction filter, which cannot distinguish repeated identical select-all
71	    dispatches; the ladder must intercept Mod-A before dispatch. Reuses
72	    `escalate.ts`'s subtree covers as the rung geometry.
73	  - *Multi-range*: each range steps its own ladder; `EditorSelection` normalization
74	    merges overlapping results, and the uniform-escalation rule keeps merged results
75	    whole-subtree-valid.
76	  - *Precedent*: generalizes both Workflowy's two-step Ctrl+A (line → whole page)
77	    and obsidian-outliner's item → list; matches Logseq's parent-by-parent
78	    escalation; degrades to the simpler behaviors in shallow documents.
79	  - *Detail to pin down at spec time*: whether a list item's "own content text" rung
80	    starts after the `- ` marker (recommended: content only, matching
81	    obsidian-outliner and reading better for copy) or at the line start.
82	  - *Why it matters beyond convenience*: it is the keyboard answer to single-node
83	    selection for tight list items, where the gap-line trigger has no geometry to
84	    work with (see next item).
85	- **Single-node selection for tight list items (no gap lines).** In a tight list the
86	  next sibling starts on the very next line — no drag gesture can mean "just this
87	  item," so the gap-line trigger cannot apply (loose lists already work: their blank
88	  lines are item-owned gaps). The keyboard path is the selection ladder above; the
89	  natural mouse path is a click-the-bullet/marker-selects-the-subtree gesture
90	  (Logseq/Workflowy bullet semantics) — a DOM/decoration-layer interaction that
91	  belongs with the decorations work (docs/research/12), not the transaction funnel.
92	- **Gap lines in general.** The manual pass surfaced a broader unease: gap lines
93	  *look* like inert empty space between blocks (no indentation, no visual ownership)
94	  but *are* owned by the preceding node in the model — the gap-line escalation
95	  trigger papers over one symptom, not the concept. Candidate directions when
96	  structural deletion (Phase C) makes gap semantics user-visible: visual gap
97	  ownership cues (a decoration-layer idea — see also docs/research/12), collapsing
98	  multi-blank gaps on structural moves, and whether cursor placement on gap lines
99	  should snap or stay free. Whatever Phase C decides about "deleting a node takes its
100	  trailing gap along" should be reconciled with how selections over gaps behave.
101	- **Escalated-selection visual treatment.** The manual pass noted selection still
102	  *renders* as character-level highlight even when escalated to whole nodes; a
103	  block-level selection indication (whole-node highlight chrome) was judged out of
104	  scope for the enforcement change — it belongs with the decoration/polish layer
105	  (docs/research/12) but becomes more valuable once Phase C makes escalated
106	  selections the operand of structural edits.

# Selection-enforcement follow-ups and Phase C pickup notes

Findings from the first real-vault manual pass of `outline-selection-enforcement`
(2026-07-20), and the deferred design threads Phase C (edit rewriting/vetoing) should
pick up. The core verdict was positive: escalation works, live-drag is stable,
within-node selection and return-to-character-level behavior feel right.

## Resolved by amendment in the same change (2026-07-20)

Two of the original findings were adopted as D4 amendments rather than deferred —
see design.md's "D4 amendments" and the amended node-selection-enforcement delta spec:

- **Single-node selection via the gap-line trigger.** A same-node selection with an
  end on a trailing gap line escalates to that one node's whole subtree — dragging
  past a node's end, before the next node, selects exactly that node. Gap *ownership*
  (trailing gap belongs to the preceding node) is unchanged.
- **Uniform multi-range escalation.** Once any range escalates, every non-empty
  in-jurisdiction range escalates to at least its own node's subtree, so a multi-range
  copy is always a concatenation of complete subtrees — the mixed
  block-level/mid-node-fragment copy observed in the manual pass can no longer occur.
- **Expand-only invariant** (a required companion to the gap-line trigger): escalation
  never shrinks a range, which keeps no-frontmatter Select All byte-identical to stock
  (and fixed a latent trailing-newline exclusion in the pre-amendment behavior).

## Known native limitation (not ours to fix)

**Drags starting inside a rendered callout/table can't escape the widget.** In Live
Preview, when the cursor is outside a callout/table, the block is an opaque
`.cm-embed-block` replacement widget: a drag starting inside its rendered content is a
browser DOM selection that never becomes a main-editor CM6 selection, so no
transaction exists for the funnel to normalize. Table cells being edited are separate
nested CM6 editors with Obsidian's own cell→row→table selection escalation,
deliberately untouched (D6 degeneracy). Confirmed stock: reproduces with outline mode
off and with the plugin disabled. Blockquotes and code fences are unaffected (they
render as real `.cm-line`s). Any fix would mean DOM-level selection interception —
the enumerate-the-inputs architecture the manifest rejects — or an upstream Obsidian
change. Mitigation that already works: sweep from outside the widget and it is
selected whole.

## Phase C pickup notes

Threads deliberately deferred from the selection change, to be picked up when the
edit-rewriting change is specified:

- **Paste-site structural handling.** The uniform multi-range rule guarantees the
  *copied* content is a valid sequence of whole subtrees, but pasting is a document
  edit: pasted block content still splices at character level into the target
  position (observed: a block-level copy pasted mid-node merges with the surrounding
  paragraph). Phase C's paste re-indentation / boundary-respecting insertion is where
  this closes.
- **Modal block-level keyboard selection.** Once a selection is escalated, keyboard
  extension (Shift+Down etc.) currently moves the underlying character cursor and
  re-escalates per transaction — which works, but a true block-selection mode would
  extend by whole sibling subtrees per keypress, at every range of a multi-range
  selection simultaneously. This is a modal-behavior design (when to enter/leave the
  mode, how it interacts with the reversible drag-back behavior the manual pass
  praised) — spec it deliberately, not as a patch on the current rule.
- **Progressive Select All (the selection ladder).** Design discussed and agreed
  after the second manual pass (2026-07-20), deliberately parked here rather than
  amended in. Repeated Cmd+A presses climb a ladder: the node's own content text →
  the node's whole subtree → the parent's subtree → … → the whole outline → the
  whole document including frontmatter (which is exactly native Select All, so the
  ladder tops out into stock behavior the filter already passes through). Design
  decisions already made:
  - *Stateless*: each press compares the current selection against the ladder's
    rungs and picks the next one up — no double-press timers; robust after any
    interruption. Same approach obsidian-outliner uses for its two-step version
    ("once = current list item, twice = entire list").
  - *Mechanism*: a high-precedence keymap handler like the grammar's Tab/Enter — NOT
    the transaction filter, which cannot distinguish repeated identical select-all
    dispatches; the ladder must intercept Mod-A before dispatch. Reuses
    `escalate.ts`'s subtree covers as the rung geometry.
  - *Multi-range*: each range steps its own ladder; `EditorSelection` normalization
    merges overlapping results, and the uniform-escalation rule keeps merged results
    whole-subtree-valid.
  - *Precedent*: generalizes both Workflowy's two-step Ctrl+A (line → whole page)
    and obsidian-outliner's item → list; matches Logseq's parent-by-parent
    escalation; degrades to the simpler behaviors in shallow documents.
  - *Detail to pin down at spec time*: whether a list item's "own content text" rung
    starts after the `- ` marker (recommended: content only, matching
    obsidian-outliner and reading better for copy) or at the line start.
  - *Why it matters beyond convenience*: it is the keyboard answer to single-node
    selection for tight list items, where the gap-line trigger has no geometry to
    work with (see next item).
- **Single-node selection for tight list items (no gap lines).** In a tight list the
  next sibling starts on the very next line — no drag gesture can mean "just this
  item," so the gap-line trigger cannot apply (loose lists already work: their blank
  lines are item-owned gaps). The keyboard path is the selection ladder above; the
  natural mouse path is a click-the-bullet/marker-selects-the-subtree gesture
  (Logseq/Workflowy bullet semantics) — a DOM/decoration-layer interaction that
  belongs with the decorations work (docs/research/12), not the transaction funnel.
- **Gap lines in general.** The manual pass surfaced a broader unease: gap lines
  *look* like inert empty space between blocks (no indentation, no visual ownership)
  but *are* owned by the preceding node in the model — the gap-line escalation
  trigger papers over one symptom, not the concept. Candidate directions when
  structural deletion (Phase C) makes gap semantics user-visible: visual gap
  ownership cues (a decoration-layer idea — see also docs/research/12), collapsing
  multi-blank gaps on structural moves, and whether cursor placement on gap lines
  should snap or stay free. Whatever Phase C decides about "deleting a node takes its
  trailing gap along" should be reconciled with how selections over gaps behave.
- **Escalated-selection visual treatment.** The manual pass noted selection still
  *renders* as character-level highlight even when escalated to whole nodes; a
  block-level selection indication (whole-node highlight chrome) was judged out of
  scope for the enforcement change — it belongs with the decoration/polish layer
  (docs/research/12) but becomes more valuable once Phase C makes escalated
  selections the operand of structural edits.

# Selection-enforcement follow-ups (two tracks)

Findings from the real-vault manual passes of `outline-selection-enforcement`
(2026-07-20). The core verdict was positive: escalation works, live-drag is stable,
within-node selection and return-to-character-level behavior feel right.

Deferred threads split into **two distinct tracks** — kept apart deliberately so
Phase C doesn't scope-creep:

- **Phase C (edit enforcement)**: rewriting/vetoing *document edits* — boundary-
  crossing deletions become structural deletions, paste re-indentation, orphan
  prevention. Exactly the scope design.md gave it; nothing selection-UX lives here.
- **Selection UX (its own future change or changes)**: richer ways to *make and see*
  node selections — the select-all ladder, modal block selection, bullet-click,
  block-level selection rendering. Builds on the shipped escalation machinery but is
  keymap/decoration work, independent of edit rewriting; it does not need to wait for
  Phase C, nor Phase C for it.

## Resolved by amendment in the same change (2026-07-20)

Two of the original findings were adopted as D4 amendments rather than deferred —
see design.md's "D4 amendments" and the amended node-selection-enforcement delta spec:

- **Single-node selection via the gap-line trigger.** A same-node selection with an
  end on a trailing gap line escalates to that one node's whole subtree — dragging
  past a node's end, before the next node, selects exactly that node. Gap *ownership*
  (trailing gap belongs to the preceding node) is unchanged.
- **Uniform multi-range escalation.** Once any range escalates, every non-empty
  in-jurisdiction range escalates to at least its own node's subtree, so a multi-range
  copy is always a concatenation of complete subtrees — the mixed
  block-level/mid-node-fragment copy observed in the manual pass can no longer occur.
- **Expand-only invariant** (a required companion to the gap-line trigger): escalation
  never shrinks a range, which keeps no-frontmatter Select All byte-identical to stock
  (and fixed a latent trailing-newline exclusion in the pre-amendment behavior).

## Known native limitation (not ours to fix)

**Drags starting inside a rendered callout/table can't escape the widget.** In Live
Preview, when the cursor is outside a callout/table, the block is an opaque
`.cm-embed-block` replacement widget: a drag starting inside its rendered content is a
browser DOM selection that never becomes a main-editor CM6 selection, so no
transaction exists for the funnel to normalize. Table cells being edited are separate
nested CM6 editors with Obsidian's own cell→row→table selection escalation,
deliberately untouched (D6 degeneracy). Confirmed stock: reproduces with outline mode
off and with the plugin disabled. Blockquotes and code fences are unaffected (they
render as real `.cm-line`s). Any fix would mean DOM-level selection interception —
the enumerate-the-inputs architecture the manifest rejects — or an upstream Obsidian
change. Mitigation that already works: sweep from outside the widget and it is
selected whole.

## Track 1: Phase C (edit enforcement) inputs

Threads that genuinely feed the edit-rewriting change:

- **Paste-site structural handling.** The uniform multi-range rule guarantees the
  *copied* content is a valid sequence of whole subtrees, but pasting is a document
  edit: pasted block content still splices at character level into the target
  position (observed: a block-level copy pasted mid-node merges with the surrounding
  paragraph). Phase C's paste re-indentation / boundary-respecting insertion is where
  this closes.
- **Gap-line deletion semantics.** Trailing gap lines are node-owned in the model but
  read as inert empty space on screen. Phase C's "deleting a node takes its trailing
  gap along" makes that ownership user-visible for the first time — whatever it
  decides must be reconciled with how selections over gaps already behave (the
  gap-line escalation trigger, expand-only retention of gap ends). Related but
  separate: *visual* gap treatment (ownership cues, cursor snapping, collapsing
  multi-blank gaps on structural moves) is decoration/UX territory — see
  docs/research/12 and Track 2.

## Track 2: Selection UX (separate future change)

Richer node-selection interactions on top of the shipped escalation core. Keymap and
decoration work — independent of Phase C:

- **Progressive Select All (the selection ladder).** Design discussed and agreed
  after the second manual pass (2026-07-20). Repeated Cmd+A presses climb a ladder:
  the node's own content text → the node's whole subtree → the parent's subtree → …
  → the whole outline → the whole document including frontmatter (which is exactly
  native Select All, so the ladder tops out into stock behavior the filter already
  passes through). Design decisions already made:
  - *Stateless*: each press compares the current selection against the ladder's
    rungs and picks the next one up — no double-press timers; robust after any
    interruption. Same approach obsidian-outliner uses for its two-step version
    ("once = current list item, twice = entire list").
  - *Mechanism*: a high-precedence keymap handler like the grammar's Tab/Enter — NOT
    the transaction filter, which cannot distinguish repeated identical select-all
    dispatches; the ladder must intercept Mod-A before dispatch. Reuses
    `escalate.ts`'s subtree covers as the rung geometry.
  - *Multi-range*: each range steps its own ladder; `EditorSelection` normalization
    merges overlapping results, and the uniform-escalation rule keeps merged results
    whole-subtree-valid.
  - *Precedent*: generalizes both Workflowy's two-step Ctrl+A (line → whole page)
    and obsidian-outliner's item → list; matches Logseq's parent-by-parent
    escalation; degrades to the simpler behaviors in shallow documents.
  - *Detail to pin down at spec time*: whether a list item's "own content text" rung
    starts after the `- ` marker (recommended: content only, matching
    obsidian-outliner and reading better for copy) or at the line start.
  - *Why it matters beyond convenience*: it is the keyboard answer to single-node
    selection for tight list items, where the gap-line trigger has no geometry to
    work with (see next item).
- **Single-node selection for tight list items (no gap lines).** In a tight list the
  next sibling starts on the very next line — no drag gesture can mean "just this
  item," so the gap-line trigger cannot apply (loose lists already work: their blank
  lines are item-owned gaps). The keyboard path is the selection ladder above; the
  natural mouse path is a click-the-bullet/marker-selects-the-subtree gesture
  (Logseq/Workflowy bullet semantics) — a DOM/decoration-layer interaction that
  belongs with the decorations work (docs/research/12), not the transaction funnel.
- **Modal block-level keyboard selection.** Once a selection is escalated, keyboard
  extension (Shift+Down etc.) currently moves the underlying character cursor and
  re-escalates per transaction — which works, but a true block-selection mode would
  extend by whole sibling subtrees per keypress, at every range of a multi-range
  selection simultaneously. This is a modal-behavior design (when to enter/leave the
  mode, how it interacts with the reversible drag-back behavior the manual pass
  praised) — spec it deliberately, not as a patch on the current rule.
- **Escalated-selection visual treatment.** The manual pass noted selection still
  *renders* as character-level highlight even when escalated to whole nodes; a
  block-level selection indication (whole-node highlight chrome) was judged out of
  scope for the enforcement change — it belongs with the decoration/polish layer
  (docs/research/12) but becomes more valuable once escalated selections are the
  operand of structural edits (Phase C) and of the ladder/modal gestures above.

  block-level selection rendering. Builds on the shipped escalation machinery but is
  keymap/decoration work, independent of edit rewriting; it does not need to wait for
  Phase C, nor Phase C for it.

## Resolved by amendment in the same change (2026-07-20)

Two of the original findings were adopted as D4 amendments rather than deferred —
see design.md's "D4 amendments" and the amended node-selection-enforcement delta spec:

- **Single-node selection via the gap-line trigger.** A same-node selection with an
  end on a trailing gap line escalates to that one node's whole subtree — dragging
  past a node's end, before the next node, selects exactly that node. Gap *ownership*
  (trailing gap belongs to the preceding node) is unchanged.
- **Uniform multi-range escalation.** Once any range escalates, every non-empty
  in-jurisdiction range escalates to at least its own node's subtree, so a multi-range
  copy is always a concatenation of complete subtrees — the mixed
  block-level/mid-node-fragment copy observed in the manual pass can no longer occur.
- **Expand-only invariant** (a required companion to the gap-line trigger): escalation
  never shrinks a range, which keeps no-frontmatter Select All byte-identical to stock
  (and fixed a latent trailing-newline exclusion in the pre-amendment behavior).

## Known native limitation (not ours to fix)

**Drags starting inside a rendered callout/table can't escape the widget.** In Live
Preview, when the cursor is outside a callout/table, the block is an opaque
`.cm-embed-block` replacement widget: a drag starting inside its rendered content is a
browser DOM selection that never becomes a main-editor CM6 selection, so no
transaction exists for the funnel to normalize. Table cells being edited are separate
nested CM6 editors with Obsidian's own cell→row→table selection escalation,
deliberately untouched (D6 degeneracy). Confirmed stock: reproduces with outline mode
off and with the plugin disabled. Blockquotes and code fences are unaffected (they
render as real `.cm-line`s). Any fix would mean DOM-level selection interception —
the enumerate-the-inputs architecture the manifest rejects — or an upstream Obsidian
change. Mitigation that already works: sweep from outside the widget and it is
selected whole.

## Track 1: Phase C (edit enforcement) inputs

Threads that genuinely feed the edit-rewriting change:

- **Paste-site structural handling.** The uniform multi-range rule guarantees the
  *copied* content is a valid sequence of whole subtrees, but pasting is a document
  edit: pasted block content still splices at character level into the target
  position (observed: a block-level copy pasted mid-node merges with the surrounding
  paragraph). Phase C's paste re-indentation / boundary-respecting insertion is where
  this closes.
- **Gap-line deletion semantics.** Trailing gap lines are node-owned in the model but
  read as inert empty space on screen. Phase C's "deleting a node takes its trailing
  gap along" makes that ownership user-visible for the first time — whatever it
  decides must be reconciled with how selections over gaps already behave (the
  gap-line escalation trigger, expand-only retention of gap ends). Related but
  separate: *visual* gap treatment (ownership cues, cursor snapping, collapsing
  multi-blank gaps on structural moves) is decoration/UX territory — see
  docs/research/12 and Track 2.

## Track 2: Selection UX (separate future change)

Richer node-selection interactions on top of the shipped escalation core. Keymap and
decoration work — independent of Phase C:

- **Progressive Select All (the selection ladder).** Design discussed and agreed
  after the second manual pass (2026-07-20). Repeated Cmd+A presses climb a ladder:
  the node's own content text → the node's whole subtree → the parent's subtree → …
  → the whole outline → the whole document including frontmatter (which is exactly
  native Select All, so the ladder tops out into stock behavior the filter already
  passes through). Design decisions already made:
  - *Stateless*: each press compares the current selection against the ladder's
    rungs and picks the next one up — no double-press timers; robust after any
    interruption. Same approach obsidian-outliner uses for its two-step version
    ("once = current list item, twice = entire list").
  - *Mechanism*: a high-precedence keymap handler like the grammar's Tab/Enter — NOT
    the transaction filter, which cannot distinguish repeated identical select-all
    dispatches; the ladder must intercept Mod-A before dispatch. Reuses
    `escalate.ts`'s subtree covers as the rung geometry.
  - *Multi-range*: each range steps its own ladder; `EditorSelection` normalization
    merges overlapping results, and the uniform-escalation rule keeps merged results
    whole-subtree-valid.
  - *Precedent*: generalizes both Workflowy's two-step Ctrl+A (line → whole page)
    and obsidian-outliner's item → list; matches Logseq's parent-by-parent
    escalation; degrades to the simpler behaviors in shallow documents.
  - *Detail to pin down at spec time*: whether a list item's "own content text" rung
    starts after the `- ` marker (recommended: content only, matching
    obsidian-outliner and reading better for copy) or at the line start.
  - *Why it matters beyond convenience*: it is the keyboard answer to single-node
    selection for tight list items, where the gap-line trigger has no geometry to
    work with (see next item).
- **Single-node selection for tight list items (no gap lines).** In a tight list the
  next sibling starts on the very next line — no drag gesture can mean "just this
  item," so the gap-line trigger cannot apply (loose lists already work: their blank
  lines are item-owned gaps). The keyboard path is the selection ladder above; the
  natural mouse path is a click-the-bullet/marker-selects-the-subtree gesture
  (Logseq/Workflowy bullet semantics) — a DOM/decoration-layer interaction that
  belongs with the decorations work (docs/research/12), not the transaction funnel.
- **Modal block-level keyboard selection.** Once a selection is escalated, keyboard
  extension (Shift+Down etc.) currently moves the underlying character cursor and
  re-escalates per transaction — which works, but a true block-selection mode would
  extend by whole sibling subtrees per keypress, at every range of a multi-range
  selection simultaneously. This is a modal-behavior design (when to enter/leave the
  mode, how it interacts with the reversible drag-back behavior the manual pass
  praised) — spec it deliberately, not as a patch on the current rule.
- **Escalated-selection visual treatment.** The manual pass noted selection still
  *renders* as character-level highlight even when escalated to whole nodes; a
  block-level selection indication (whole-node highlight chrome) was judged out of
  scope for the enforcement change — it belongs with the decoration/polish layer
  (docs/research/12) but becomes more valuable once escalated selections are the
  operand of structural edits (Phase C) and of the ladder/modal gestures above.
